오디오명: Where Did Silla’s Glass Come From
대본내용: [Narration]Along with gold items, glass vessels were another luxury item of the Silla period. Since glass vessels were more expensive than gold, they were only used by the upper classes.[Visitor]Glass was more expensive than gold That’s difficult to believe…[Narration]Yes, it was. Glass was first introduced to Korea during the Three Kingdoms Period. Such beautiful glass became popular among the nobility, but the manufacturers of Silla weren’t highly skilled and couldn’t keep up with the demand. [Narration]Wait, are you serious The glass vessels here are beautiful. I can’t believe there was a lack of quality workmanship. The colors and patterns are as good as those I have seen in Western countries.[Narration]No wonder, because this is Roman glassware.[Visitor]Wow! How can that be[Narration]This glassware is believed to have been made in the Mediterranean region and imported into Silla. The shape of the vessels and the components of the glass are different from the glassware made in Silla or China and are much more similar to Roman glass.[Visitor]Can it be true that glassware was transported all the way from Rome to the far east of Asia Was that possible[Narration]Yes, it was. Historians believe that merchants of Central Asia imported glassware along the Silk Road, a route that crossed the northern grasslands of China. Trade between Silla and other countries was very active at that time.

스팟명: Ornament Sheath of Precious Dagger(Scabbard Ornament)
오디오명: Ornament Sheath of Precious Dagger, Trace of Interchange and Communication
대본내용: The treasure in the corner inevitably catches one’s eye with its dazzling brilliance. It is the 1,500-year-old ornamental sheath of a precious dagger. At first sight, it looks exotic and unlike any of the other treasures. The unique patterns on the sheath and the wave pattern and yin-yang symbols on the surface are dissimilar to Korean traditional motifs. Also, the jewels and glass decorating it were a very rare type of decoration in the Silla kingdom. This is because this dagger was imported by way of the Silk Road like the glass vessels we saw earlier. The origin of the dagger remained a mystery for a long time. The artistic technique using gold and colorful jewels is found across a broad swath of Europe, from Germany to West Siberia, but the exact providence of the item is unknown.However, after 37 years of research, new information was uncovered and historians reached a temporary conclusion. First, the iron sword hidden in the sheath is a double-bladed dagger, which was mainly made in Middle Asia. Second, the red jewels on the right protrusion are garnets, which are commonly found in Eastern Europe. Thus, this dagger almost certainly was imported into Silla along the Silk Road. The dagger is proof of the vigorous exchange and communication Silla maintained with other parts of the world.

스팟명: Monument to the Martyrdom of Ichadon
오디오명: Execution Site and a Rain of Flowers
대본내용: This is the Monument to the Martyrdom of Ichadon made in the 6th century. Ichadon contributed to the propagation of Buddhism throughout the Silla kingdom. Just as early Christians were persecuted for their beliefs, it was initially difficult for Buddhism to gain acceptance in Silla.When Buddhism was first introduced to Silla, the provincial nobility persecuted Buddhists with the aim of preserving their own folk religion. Nevertheless, Ichadon built a Buddhist temple and was subsequently executed. At the moment of his death, he predicted that a miracle from Buddha would occur and became a martyr for Buddhism.In front of the monument, there is a sculpture depicting the martyrdom of Ichadon. Let’s have a look. Can you see his resolute expression Instead of red blood, the sculpture shows white blood rising up to the sky. The wave pattern under his feet symbolizes the earth itself shaking and mourning his death. The rain of flowers falling from heaven honors his noble sacrifice. Deeply moved by the martyrdom of Ichadon, the people of Silla started to accept Buddhism as a valid religion. This is how Buddhism became the national religion of Silla, and even today Buddhism remains one of the most popular religions in Korea.

These four metal plates were found under the pole of the pagoda. Originally, they formed a metal box joined by hinges. Now they are separate pieces. Can you guess what the box was forIt was to hold the Buddha’s sarira. Cremation is a Buddhist funeral rite. After the cremation of a Buddha, or a person who had attained enlightenment, small crystalline fragments remained in the ashes. These rare relics, known as sariras, are kept in important temples in a box inside the pagoda. Christians have similar rituals to preserve the bodies of saints in churches.This box has additional historic value. Three of the four plates are inscribed with records detailing the history of the pagoda. The nine-story, 80-meter-high structure was the tallest wooden pagoda in Korean history. Unfortunately, the original structure was burnt down by Mongol invaders in 1238. However, thanks to the excavation of Chaljubongi, we can clearly identify detailed information about exactly when, how, and by whom the pagoda was constructed and repaired.

스팟명: Stone Monument Inscribed with the Hwarang Oath
오디오명: Stone Monument Inscribed with the Hwarang Oath, a Pledge of Allegiance
대본내용: The inscription on this small stone monument is a pledge of allegiance written to renew the writers’ determination to train their minds and bodies to contribute to the kingdom. This was written by two young men of Silla believed to be the members of the Hwarangdo. The Hwarangdo is an elite group of male youth who were both intelligent and courageous. It was a voluntary organization dedicated to training the body and the mind, similar to the Boy Scouts movement of today. The Hwarangdo was founded while Silla was preparing for war, right before it unified the three kingdoms of the Korean Peninsula. After finishing their training, some members of the Hwarangdo entered the military and fought with distinction on the battlefield. Notably, the members of the Hwarangdo were only 15 to 18 years old. These young men spent almost three years training in various locations around the country. Still boys, it must have been very tough having to undergo intense training away from home for such a long period of time.The handwriting is quite crude and rough, yet powerful. It symbolizes the patriotic spirit of the Silla kingdom, the first unifier of the Korean Peninsula. Inspired by the spirit of the Hwarangdo, many youth groups and programs for youth in Korea today bear the name “Hwarang".

[Narration]Now, shall we look around Gyeongju as it was 1,300 years ago Gyeongju, the capital of Silla, was a flourishing city and may have been comparable to the ancient cities of Constantinople, Baghdad and Chang’an. Well, what do you notice first[Visitor]The houses look identical. They are all single-storied and have roofs of the same color.[Narration]Yes, you are right. All the houses have tiled roofs. During the Three Kingdoms Period, tile-roofed houses were built all over the Korean Peninsula. Did you notice that the houses are arranged in rows and form a grid pattern This is because Gyeongju was a well-planned city. According to legend, the people of Gyeongju didn’t need an umbrella when it rained, as they were sheltered by the eaves of the houses. It was like walking under the arcades of Bologna.[Visitor]Where is the palace by the way I can’t find it. The buildings all look so similar.[Narration]Do you see the crescent-shaped piece of land near the river It is called Wolseong, meaning moon-shaped palace. Now, try to find the main street that runs from north to south. In China and Korea, it was common to have a wide road leading to the entrance of the palace. The biggest palace in Seoul, Gyeongbok Palace, also has a broad street leading to its main gate. Also, you can see there is a river to the south which functions as a natural moat.[Visitor]What is the towering building on the right The palace consists only of single story buildings, but that one is as tall as the spire of a cathedral.[Narration]That building was also built by order of the king. It is the biggest temple of Silla and the top of the building is the temple’s pagoda. Buddhism was the national religion of Silla and many temples were built by royal command.

오디오명: Live Forever to Protect My Country
대본내용: What do you think this broken rock is If you take a closer look, you will see that it is a monument with inscriptions on the surface. The monument was erected to pay tribute to a Silla king Munmu and his great achievements. In ancient Korea, there were three kingdoms, including Silla. The king ended the long-standing conflict between the three kingdoms and united the Peninsula.In accordance with his will, no tomb was built for King Munmu in Gyeongju. King Munmu had always worried about Japanese invaders crossing the East Sea and terrorizing Silla, so in his will he ordered that his body be cremated and his ashes scattered over the East Sea so that he could become a dragon and defend Silla forever. His wishes were respected, and a large rocky island off the coast near Gyeongju is regarded as his tomb.His tombstone was erected in a temple in Gyeongju. The temple was built to honor and gain favor with Buddha, in order that Silla might be victorious in the war immediately preceding unification. The temple held great meaning for the king, and thus his tombstone was appropriately placed there.On the front side of this tombstone, King Munmu’s achievements and the process of the unification of three kingdoms are described. On the back are inscribed the king’s will and eulogies. The tombstone in front of you is the lower half of the original one. The original tombstone was broken into two and the upper half remained missing until recently. But the missing upper half was discovered in 2009 and is currently under preservation process which will hopefully be completed soon.

스팟명: Four Guardian Kings of Nogyu Temple
오디오명: Heavenly Guardian Kings who Protected Silla
대본내용: [Narration]These pieces are bricks depicting the Buddhist Heavenly Guardians, which are similar to guardian angels in Christianity. Do you see the devils under the guardians The Buddhist Guardians were traditionally carved at the entrance to temples and served as gatekeepers.[Visitor]But why are they in fragments[Narration]The bricks were found smashed into 200 small pieces and they remained that way for many years. The lower half of the body was excavated in 1936, but the upper part was only discovered 70 years later. Because several different excavation teams had worked on the project over many years, the fragments had been kept in five different institutes separately. But in 2009, they were finally assembled and a special exhibition was held to showcase the artifact. There is a picture of it in the middle.[Visitor]Wow, even though it’s broken, it’s still very beautiful. I hope the missing parts are found soon.[Narration]I agree. You can imagine the strong heavenly guardians bringing comfort to the people of Silla. People suffered during the long drawn-out wars and Buddhism provided relief and spiritual sanctuary. Just as Europeans fought for their nations buoyed by their Christian beliefs, the people of Silla united and defended their kingdom supported by Buddhism. [Visitor]I understand. These heavenly guardians symbolized the population’s hope and desire for peace.[Narration]Yes, they did. Even today, there are still many Buddhist temple sites and remnants of Buddhist statues and pagodas in the Gyeongju area. Most of them were built for the purpose of praying for peace and comfort.

[Narration]These clay figurines are called toyong in Korean. They are similar to tou, the smaller clay figurines we saw earlier, but toyong expressed Silla people’s appearance and their garments more exquisitely. After tou gradually disappeared during the late 6th century, the more detailed toyong gained popularity.[Visitor]Does that mean craftsmen became more skilled at making the figurines[Narration]Not really. Don’t you remember the mounted warrior kettle Skilled craftsmen and advanced techniques were readily available and in use. Toyong were influenced by the style of Chinese dolls. In ancient China, realistic life-sized dolls were traditionally buried with the dead.In Silla, when a person of high social standing died, his remaining family and servants were buried with him. Actually, a similar practice was found all over the world, including in ancient Europe. Silla prohibited this cruel custom in the early 6th century, and thereafter, toyong were used to replace the family and servants of the dead.

[Narration]The first exhibit in the Art Hall is ‘the Smile of Silla’. If you look around downtown Gyeongju, you will see this face everywhere. How do you think this small, broken figurine became a famous symbol of Silla[Visitor]Was it made by a famous artist from the Silla kingdom[Narration]Maybe. But there’s no information about who made it, because it is just a common earthen roof tile. It was meant to be a building material, not a piece of art.[Visitor]Then it must have come from a very famous building, like the Hanging Gardens of Babylon for example.[Narration]That is interesting reasoning. However, this roof tile is famous for its face design, rather than for where it came from. Korean traditional roof tiles were usually decorated with lotus patterns, so the face design is very rare.[Visitor]I see. In ancient Europe, faces of saints or devils are popular in architectural decoration. But smiling faces are rarely found. How interesting![Narration] Look at the face on the roof tile and you will understand why it has received so much attention. The smile of this Silla woman has a pure, natural beauty. Elongated eyes, a round nose and a mysterious demure smile… it can be compared to the smile of Mona Lisa!

오디오명: The World of Buddha Statues- Buddha and Buddhist Saints
대본내용: Before walking into the next big exhibition room, let’s visit the small room on the right. The small statues in this room will help you to understand and appreciate the variety and symbolism of Buddha statues from the Silla kingdom. These Buddhist saints practice the teachings of Buddha and lead people, and can be understood as a similar concept to the twelve apostles of Jesus. They are usually wearing jeweled crowns and accessories such as bracelets and brooches. To encourage a sense of familiarity and intimacy, they look more like humans than Buddha. You will see many different statues of Buddha in different poses. ‘Buddha’ means an ‘enlightened one’. Unlike the monotheistic God of the Christian religion, in Buddhism any enlightened person can be a Buddha. Therefore, there are many different Buddhas. For example, there is a Buddha whose role is to guide people to heaven and a Buddha who cures disease and ignorance.The four Buddhas in the glass case on the right are known as the VairocanaBuddhas, who symbolize the truths of Buddhism. The identifying characteristic of the VairocanaBuddhas is their hand gesture. The Buddha holds an index finger with the other hand, which signifies that foolishness and enlightenment come from the same root. The statues in the glass case by the entrance are of Buddha and the Buddhist saints together. Of these, the small broken statue is the most famous one. They are standing together surrounded by the Buddha’s halo. On the right is a statue in a unique pose. The Buddha is sitting on a lotus-shaped throne, with the right leg resting on the left knee. The right hand is placed on the chin. Doesn’t it bear a resemblance to Auguste Rodin’s ‘The Thinker’ Both depict a man in sober meditation.

This room is filled with tall statues of Buddha. Buddhism was the national religion of the Silla kingdom, and Buddha statues of varying sizes were crafted throughout the years. Statues of Buddha and temple architecture were a major part of Silla’s artistic tradition.This Buddha statue in particular is aesthetically outstanding and one of the most famous Buddhist relics from the Silla kingdom. It is designated as a National Treasure of Korea. Let’s look at it in detail. The well-shaped body under the thin robe gives an impression of weight and solidity and the solemn face expresses quiet dignity.The copper statue was originally gold-plated, a symbolic technique designed to represent the golden light of Buddha. As time passed, the gold plating eroded away and now only rusty copper remains. The statue is of the Medicine Buddha, the deity who cures the illnesses and ignorance of humans. The hands are now missing, but they were supposed to have held a small earthenware jar containing medicines.

오디오명: Hwangnyong Temple with Golden Guardian Dragon
대본내용: [Narration]What do you think this huge vertical structure is Any guesses[Visitor]Well, this is the Hall of Hwangnyong Temple, so it must be something related to the temple, right Is that a statue in the temple yard It looks like an abstract sculpture.[Narration]This is called a chimi(mangsae), which means a ridge-end ornament tile. It was originally on the corner of the roof at the main hall of Hwangnyong Temple. The dovetail design is believed to keep evil spirits away, just like the gargoyles of gothic cathedrals in the Middle Ages.[Visitor]Awesome! But isn’t it too big to put on the roof It’s huge![Narration]Hwangnyong Temple was the biggest temple in Silla, so its main hall was huge. This tile is famous for being the biggest and most brilliant ridge-end ornament tile made during the Three Kingdoms Period. And it has another interesting feature. Take a look at the back of the tile and tell me what you see.[Visitor]Oh, the faces! How wonderful![Narration]Yes, you are right. Small, smiling faces are hidden there as if playing hide-and-seek. You can see the passion and delicacy of Silla artisans in the intricate details of the tile.

오디오명: Wooden Pagoda of Hwangnyong Temple, the Nine-storied Guardian against Invasion by the Nine Neighboring Nations
대본내용: This miniature is a replica of Hwangnyong Temple, which was the largest Buddhist temple of Silla. The lofty tower looking down over the temple is quite impressive. Hwangnyong Temple was built to protect and defend the nation using the power of Buddha. There is a legend about the construction of the temple. Whilst builders were working on a palace extension project, a golden dragon appeared from heaven. The king considered the appearance a sign from Buddha and ordered the buildings to be reconstructed as a temple. The golden dragon became known as the protective guardian spirit of the kingdom, keeping it safe from invasion. ‘Hwangnyong’ means ‘golden dragon’, so the name of the temple is also derived from the story.The most significant structure of the temple is definitely the central pagoda. The nine-story wooden pagoda had outstanding architectural and artistic value. This tall, splendid pagoda was constructed to protect Silla against invasion by any one of its nine neighboring countries. Standing 80 meters in height, the pagoda was visible from all over Gyeongju. Even with modern technology, it would not be easy to build an 80-meter-high wooden pagoda strong enough to sustain its own weight and withstand strong winds, so this tower was an advanced architectural triumph.According to a legend, Silla asked its neighboring countries for carpenters to help build the pagoda. A particular carpenter decided to work on the project, but during the night following his decision, he dreamt about the collapse of his own country. When he awoke from his dream, he refused to continue working on the Silla pagoda. Suddenly, the heavens and the earth shook and trembled, and an old monk and a strong man suddenly appeared. They built the columns of the pagoda and, just as suddenly, disappeared. The carpenter realized that finishing the pagoda was his destiny, and so he completed the project.Unfortunately, we can’t see the original wooden pagoda now because it was burnt down during the Mongol Invasion in 1238.

오디오명: Anap Pond, the Fancy Pond of the Crown Prince
대본내용: Anap Pond was an artificial pond in the palace of the Crown Prince. The pond was built in the golden days of Silla, right after the unification of the three kingdoms. It was constructed in the grounds where open-air banquets for foreign envoys and nobles were held. Korean traditional architecture placed great emphasis on harmony with nature. Buildings were designed and arranged to complement the beauty of the natural environment. Although Anap Pond was an artificial pond, it was designed to look natural.Interestingly, this pond was constructed to remind one of the ocean. The shape of the pond was meandering, and a small islet was made in the pond. Given its shape and the presence of the islet, the other end of the pond was invisible, which led observers of the scene to feel as if they were looking at the ocean.Furthermore, the garden surrounding Anap Pond followed the tenets of Taoism, an East Asian philosophy that espouses withdrawal from reality and values meditative, peaceful environments. The twelve peaks around the pond were symbols of Taoist theology. The site of this pond is located near the museum. We recommend you pay it a visit and spend some time there. You can see the classical grace of Silla, and the view at night is especially beautiful.

오디오명: Rebirth of Silla Boat - Wooden Boat Discovered in Anap Pond
대본내용: The wooden boat in the middle of the exhibition hall was recovered from Anap Pond. The pond was large enough to accommodate the boats of Silla nobles for whom boating was a popular leisure activity. But how was the boat preserved in such good condition When Anap Pond was excavated, the water was drained from the pond. Many treasures had been well preserved in the oxygen-free mud layer at the bottom. This wooden boat was one of them. Before the boat could be displayed here, there were many complications to overcome. The boat was found in an upturned position in the mud. Because it had been underwater for such a long time, the boat’s frame had become as soft as sponge. The excavation team struggled to find a way to remove it safely without damaging the relic. It was important to protect the boat from drying out and disintegrating. After much planning, the team disassembled the boat into three parts, covered each with wet cloth, and successfully extracted them from the mud.The restoration process did not become easier once the boat had been moved to the museum. In order to be exhibited, the softened wooden boat needed to be restored to its original state. A chemical treatment process was applied in an underground warehouse to harden the timber. It took almost nine years  as many years as it took for the dust and soot to be stripped away from Michelangelo’s painting in the Sistine Chapel.

오디오명: Wooden Document, an Alternative to Paper
대본내용: The relic on the steps to the second floor is a type of note pad from the Silla kingdom. Thin, small pieces of wood were used to write on instead of paper.Wood wasn’t used for writing simply because there was no paper. As we know, paper was first invented in China, and Silla, being geographically close to China, began to use paper hundreds of years earlier than Europe. However, paper was a luxury in those times and so people used wood to write memos. Wood was also practical to reuse after sanding to erase the text.The content of the memos is simple but varied and supplies valuable information about the daily lives of the people of Silla. For example, the names of the palace gates are written on wooden fragments of key rings. There are also “time cards” of the palace guards. By combining these pieces of information, we can glean an understanding of how palace security was managed.There are also wooden pieces inscribed with lists of medicinal plants and prescriptions. From this information, we know how knowledgeable the pharmacists of Silla were. There are other pieces which record the names of fermented foods and their dates of production. Recording the date was crucial to knowing exactly when a food would be ready to eat. These notes provide information about the diet and food preparation methods of the time.

스팟명: Gilt-bronze Dragon Head Statue, Bronze Spoon and Other Relics
오디오명: Metal Craft Found from Anap Pond, Bountiful Life of the Royal Family
대본내용: These exhibits on the second floor of Anapji Hall are all metal-crafted objects that belonged to members of the Silla royal family. Most of the relics exhibited in museums were recovered from tombs but these from Anap Pond were designed for daily use.Let’s look at the tableware first. The hollow bowl with a lid was used as a rice bowl. The same small characters engraved on the bottom of the bowl and inside the lid indicate a matching set. The objects used by the royal family were much more detailed than those used by commoners. Look at the spoons in the glass case on the right. How did people eat their meals with such large utensils In fact, these were used as ladles to serve food out of large dishes.Spoons like these have been found in Japan. A boxed set of ten Silla spoons found in Japan indicates that Silla exported goods there.Stepping to the right, you will see various relics that once decorated Silla’s palaces. The king’s chair is decorated with carvings of dragon heads with bulging eyeballs and curved fangs. The dragon and the phoenix were the traditional symbols of the king in Korea, therefore many dragon and phoenix motifs would have been seen in palace interiors.

오디오명: What was the Gilt-bronze Snuffer of Anap Pond used for
대본내용: [Narration]This is a gilt bronze candle snuffer that was used by the Silla royal family. It is too big to hold in one hand, and looks quite different to a modern candle snuffer.[Visitor]Yes, it looks more like scissors.[Narration]Indeed. In a way, this functioned as a scissors. Silla peoples used it to trim the wick of a candle. They cut off the burned part of the wick with this snuffer and the cut wick fell onto the ice cream scoop-like part of it. [Visitor]Interesting! They handled candles with much care.[Narration]It is probably because the candles were rare. Not many candles were produced, and even in Europe candles were expensive and only found in palaces and famous churches in those days.

오디오명: Buddha Triad Holding the Wishes of the Royal Family
대본내용: These cast metal pieces are also statues of Buddha. The flat gilt-bronze pieces were made throughout East Asia. This one, from Anap Pond, has been praised for its artistic value. The statues were delicately made. First, a wax mold was crafted and then covered with mud and dried. Then the mold was heated to melt and remove the wax, leaving a hard clay cast. Molten bronze was then poured into the cast. This complicated procedure produced sculptures of high quality, compared to the Japanese and Chinese method of hammering metal plates. You can see the details up close. As you can see, many statues of Buddha were excavated from Anap Pond, but the one in the middle is the only complete piece among them. Its discovery implies that other Buddha triads would also have been made in a similarly delicate manner. The Buddha in the middle of the triad has a toned body and a solemn facial expression. The two disciples at Buddha’s sides are very attractive, twisting at the waist. Why do you think these triads were found in the palace garden and not at a temple site Just as Europeans had chapels inside palaces, the Silla palace had a Buddhist sanctum, and it is thought that the triads were decorative. Their function may have been to remind the royal family to pray for the peace of the nation.

오디오명: Wooden Dice, a Toy for Silla Nobility
대본내용: [Narration]The unique wooden object sitting amongst the other articles… what do you think it is Yes, it is a dice. Silla nobles used to play games with them. [Woman]Wow, really It looks so different.[Narration]Yes, it is. Modern dice are cubic, but this one has 14 faces. It has characters on each face instead of numbers.[Woman]What did they do with it[Narration]When the nobles of Silla held banquets here at Anap Pond, they played games with dice. Just as westerners played such games as backgammon, so Silla people played dice games 1,000 years ago.[Woman]It is interesting that the East also had ancient dice games. What do the characters mean[Narration]They represent forfeits for the person throwing the dice. For example, one says to drink three glasses of wine without stopping, another to dance without music, and another to be tickled.[Woman]How funny! I should try playing that with my friends.[Narration]Good. These games probably helped to enliven many parties

[Narration]The last exhibition consists of animal bones from Anap Pond. Four hundred and thirty-four pieces of bone from livestock and wild animals such as mountain goats and boars were found there.[Visitor]Shouldn’t they be kept in the Natural History Museum[Narration]Normally, yes. But there is a reason for displaying the bones here. They are evidence of the lavish lifestyle enjoyed by the nobility of Silla.[Visitor]Animal bones and a lavish lifestyle… I don’t get it.[Narration]According to historical records, the nobles of Silla kept imported and wild birds and animals here. The European nobility of the Middle Ages also collected rare animals. [Visitor]It sounds like they raised very expensive pets.[Narration]It would be more accurate to say that they created a kind of zoo near Anap Pond because the animals were mainly there to be seen and admired. The king of Silla used the animals to show off his wealth at banquets when nobles and foreign envoys were in attendance.[Visitor]It seems strange that even cows and pigs were displayed in this way.[Narration]Some of them were for eating of course. One of the wooden memos records a menu of fermented stingray, pig and roe deer. Such a varied diet was only available to the wealthy nobility and royalty.

The huge Divine Bell of King Seongdeok hanging outside the museum was made in 771. The bell was commissioned by King Seongdeok to commemorate his father, and took almost 20 years to make.The bell is famous for its beautiful design and intricate patterns. The flying angel design on the surface is particularly beautiful. Look at the praying angel under the winds of heaven. His robes are fluttering in the wind. Silla people believed that angels rode on clouds in the sky, and on the bell the angel is depicted wrapped in a flower-shaped cloud. The king’s devotion to his father is manifested in this masterpiece of the 8th century.The deep, full sound of the bell is internationally recognized for its originality. The sound you hear through the museum speaker system comes from this divine bell. Scientists have studied the bell in an attempt to replicate the beautiful sound quality, but so far it has proved impossible to imitate, even with modern technological methods.The bell was used differently from western bells. In the west, bells are normally found at the top of church spires or bell towers to direct the sound towards the heavens. This bell was hung low, almost touching the ground, to spread the sound low and wide. It is believed to reflect the horizontal world view of Buddhism, the national religion of Silla.

[Narration]Leaving the stone pagodas behind, let’s move to the back of the museum. There are tens of stone Buddhas standing in a line. After the unification of the three kingdoms, many stone Buddhas were made.[Visitor]None of these stone Buddhas have heads. [Narration]That’s true, and they were found in ponds, not in Buddhist sanctuaries. [Visitor]I don’t suppose they were made without heads.[Narration]Of course not. They were originally complete, but at some point their heads were removed. Why do you think this happened Any guesses[Boy Student]Well, an earthquake could have done the damage, or maybe they were damaged during a war. [Narration]Possibly, but historians believe that they were most likely vandalized by those in power during the 14th century. The governing group of the Korean Peninsula at that time was strongly influenced by Chinese Confucianism. They persecuted Buddhists and suppressed the Buddhist religion, which had been established in Korea for over 1,000 years. They destroyed many Buddhist temples and statues.[Boy Student]It seems impossible to have peacefully co-existing religions, no matter where you are in the world.[Narration]I agree. The new ruling group seized power in a coup. So they might have thought that introduction of a new belief system would help strengthen their political legitimacy.

스팟명: Three-story Stone Pagoda of Goseon Temple and Other Relics
오디오명: Stone Pagoda, Beautiful Mercy of Buddha
대본내용: A pair of pagodas stands outside the Archaeology Hall. These are a reproduction of the pagodas from Bulguk Temple, one of the most famous tourist destinations in Gyeongju. The stone pagodas in front of us represent the Buddhist pagodas of the Silla Kingdom.The purpose of the pagodas differs from that of western bell towers. During the Middle Ages, Europeans built bell towers as lookouts or to be closer to heaven. Buddhist pagodas, however, were made to enshrine the sariras of Buddha, so India was the first country to establish these types of pagodas. The pagodas in India resemble big upside-down bowls, whilst the towers of East Asia look like stacked cubes. The material used to construct the pagodas differs by region: mud brick in China, wood in Japan and stone in Korea.Wooden pagodas were initially popular in Silla, but because of construction difficulties and their susceptibility to fire damage, they were superseded by stone pagodas. From the late 7th century onwards, stone pagodas were built with the same design and construction methods as the original wooden pagodas.

오디오명: Cutting Edge Hunting Methods of the Prehistoric Age
대본내용: Welcome to Gimhae National Museum. The first relics you will see are hunting tools from the Prehistoric Age. Because human culture changed along with the pace of tool development, tools had a significant effect on civilization. Among them, the development of hunting tools was a decisive factor in the survival of mankind and the development of civilizations. You can see here the progression of hunting technology throughout the Prehistoric Age. Let’s start from the exhibits on the right. These are primitive tools such as stones for chopping and pointed stones. On the left are arrowheads and they were advanced weapons of the Neolithic Age.People of the Paleolithic Age initially hunted animals using simple wooden and stone tools. They then began to make spears, which made hunting safer. Tool development accelerated around about 10,000 B.C., following the end of the Ice Age. Mammals such as mammoth and reindeer were well adapted to cold climates and moved to the north, while smaller animals such as foxes and rabbits thrived. The proliferation of these small, fast animals prompted the development of long range weapons such as bows and arrows.Now let’s look at the piece of earthenware in the glass case behind you. The Neolithic artwork is inscribed with a boar motif and expresses a desire for successful boar hunting expeditions.Since the agricultural practices of the time were primitive at best, food supply primarily depended on the success of a hunt. Neolithic drawings of animals in caves or motifs on pottery expressed a desire for successful hunting. Murals discovered in Lascaux in France and Altamira in Spain were motivated by the same desires.

오디오명: A Warm-hearted Table of the Neolithic Age
대본내용: The exhibits in this glass case offer an explanation of the eating habits of the Neolithic people. The quern, which is shaped like an ironing board, and the baguette-shaped pestle were particularly significant tools of the Neolithic Age. They were used to shell or grind nuts such as acorns. Acorn cookies and jelly were staple foods of the Neolithic people who inhabited the Korean Peninsula. Following the invention of ground stone tools, the diet of Neolithic people diversified. Since ground tools were more effective than the chipped stone tools of the Paleolithic Age, Neolithic people could accomplish more complex tasks. Various kinds of earthenware were produced in order to cook and store food. These sudden changes were the result of a transition from a nomadic to a sedentary lifestyle. The population increased dramatically around 6,000 years B.C and Neolithic people began to settle down in groups. During the early period of the Neolithic Age, agriculture was non-existent, and so people still lived by hunting and gathering. As the groups of people developed into villages and communities, they were collectively able to gather large quantities of fruits and grains.

오디오명: Waves of Change and a Drawn Net
대본내용: [Narration] Thousands of years ago, grains and meat were not the only sources of food for Neolithic people. As Gimhae was surrounded by rivers and the sea, the people who inhabited this region enjoyed a diet supplemented with virtually every type of fish and marine food.[Tourist] Every type of fish and marine food! During the Neolithic Age! That must be an exaggeration.[Narration] No, not at all! The remains of fish, dolphins and even seals were identified from food waste heaps dating from the Neolithic Age. [Tourist] It’s funny that we have learned about ancient people from their trash.[Narration] Such trash heaps have revealed information about the diet of the Neolithic people, and also the fishing methods they employed. These net sinkers and bone fishhooks were found in food waste heaps as well.[Tourist] Are those fishhooks that I can see on the right Sometimes I go fishing myself, so although they look clumsy I can see that they would work!  [Narration] Yes. By using the divided fishhooks shown at the top, even large fish such as tuna and shark could be caught. Net fishing has also been in use from as long as 3,000 years ago. Just as communities of people worked together to gather food, the scale of fishing increased by their working together cooperatively. The artifact on your left is a weight. As we saw earlier, it was attached to a net to help it sink so that fish could not escape.[Tourist] Neolithic people were more intelligent than I thought. [Narration] They were intelligent. Although these artifacts are from the Neolithic Age, they are not primitive at all. They are functionally comparable to the modern fishing equipment used today. Now you know that the assertion that Neolithic people enjoyed all kinds of marine foods is not an exaggeration!

오디오명: Fashionable Pottery  Burnished Red Pottery and Pottery with sn Eggplant Motif
대본내용: The earthenware in the corner of the exhibition room was produced on the Korean Peninsula during the late Bronze Age. The style of pottery underwent a small change between the 3rd century B.C. and the 1st century A.D. There were almost no changes in the features and appearance of the pottery; however, there was a gradual development of the techniques and materials employed in the manufacturing process.The pottery we have seen so far is known as ‘plain pottery’ due to the absence of any decoration.Compared to early, crude, plain pottery, these pieces were produced using more complicated techniques. They were made of fine clay and a layer of colored mud. This process required an additional firing, and produced pottery with a smooth texture and vivid color. The bright red earthenware on the right is called ‘burnished red pottery’. The pottery on the left is known as ‘pottery with an eggplant design’ due to the black patterns on the vessels.The change in appearance of the pottery was inspired by a change in purpose. This type of earthenware was designed not only for everyday use, but also as a funerary accessory. The men of power at that time put a greater emphasis on the external beauty of earthenware in order to highlight their prestige. Thus this pottery provides evidence of the existence of a hierarchy in Bronze Age society.

오디오명: The Best Example of Stone-Craft, Polished Stone Daggers
대본내용: [Narration] How did you know that they were decorative daggers[Tourist] Do you mean that Bronze Age people were interested in interior decoration That’s unbelievable![Narration] Ah, I mean that the daggers were designed to decorate tombs, not houses.
