Some of these people are clothed in simple garments and are shoeless, whereas others have lots of body hair, and still others are dressed in nice clothes. [Tourist]It is interesting because all the faces of the people in the painting have different expressions. Is it from the Baekje Dynasty[Narration]No, this picture is from the Liang Dynasty of China. It is a picture of the envoys that traveled to China from various other countries. It was painted from a Chinese perspective and so it provides an objective view of how the neighboring countries of China were perceived. [Tourist]Then there must be people from Baekje in this picture [Narration]Yes, the arrow indicates the envoy from Baekje.[Tourist]Oh, here! I found it! He is wearing a crown on his head and a traditional Korean overcoat and trousers with leather shoes. Overall, he looks refined and elegant. [Narration]Yes. He is different from the envoys from other countries that are standing next to him. The painting implies that Baekje was a highly sophisticated kingdom compared to its neighbors. [Tourist]I guess you could say that. What does the writing on the side mean[Narration]It is a brief description about Baekje and its exchange with China. It also says the people of Baekje held their arms by their sides when walking and kept one leg bent when bowing. This painting provides invaluable and detailed information about Baekje culture and customs in the 6th century.

스팟명: Standing Gilt-bronze Buddhist Goddess of Mercy
오디오명: Standing Gilt-bronze Buddhist Goddess of Mercy
대본내용: How about we take a look at a Buddhist figure that characterized the religion of the people of Baekje The small figure you are looking at now is a Buddhist saint made out of gilt bronze. Buddhist saints practice the teachings of Buddha and lead people, and can be understood as a similar concept to the twelve apostles of Jesus. They are usually wearing jeweled crowns and accessories such as bracelets and brooches. To encourage a sense of familiarity and intimacy, they look more like humans than Buddha. This saint is, the Buddhist Goddess of Mercy.  The Buddhist Goddess of Mercy listened to all the voices and cries of people that were in pain and granted them mercy. Mother Mary is an equivalent figure in Christianity. The goddess has a chubby, smiling face, a slim figure, and hair falling down to her shoulders in two braids, one on each side. The lotus flower she holds in her right hand is an emblem of new life and the bottle in her left hand contains reviving medicine. Buddhist saints were ordained in all regions including India, the birthplace of Buddhism, and in Thailand and China as well. Buddhist figures made in India resembled Indian people and those from Thailand resembled Thai people. It was natural that people made statues of Buddhist saints in their own likenesses. Looking at the chubby cheeks and the fading smile of the statue, you can try to imagine the appearance of the people of Baekje.

[Narration]This artifact is a pedestal of Buddha that was made in the early 7th century. It is a reproduction made from pieces that were discovered at a former kiln site of the Baekje Dynasty. [Tourist]It is really big! How come there is no Buddha on the pedestal[Narration] The Buddha was not found, only the fragments of the pedestal were. The pedestal is around 1m in height and 2.8m in width. [Tourist] Oh, I see. I cannot imagine how amazing the Buddha placed on the stand must have been, since the pedestal itself is so large and majestic. It is such a pity that we cannot see what the Buddha looked like. [Narration]I agree with you. The flowing appearance of Buddha's garments draping over the stand must have been beautiful. The artisans who carved the statue in order to make it look like flowing, draped cloth must have been highly skilled. It's impressive. [Tourist]That is really true. The carved folds really do make it look like flowing cloth. [Narration] The Buddhist art in Baekje was of an extraordinarily high quality. This you can assume just by looking at the pedestal of Buddha. This is also why the Buddhist art from Baekje influenced Japanese art. You will notice that many famous temples and statues in Japan are similar to those of Baekje.

[Narration]This is the tombstone and its rubbing for a person named Ye from Baekje. [Tourist] He must have been a very important person then for his tombstone and the rubbing to be exhibited. [Narration]Well…Ye was a general of Baekje. However, after the collapse of Baekje in 660 A.D., he went to China and served as a highly ranked official. So, his tombstone was discovered in China in 2006.If you look at the inscription on the tombstone, you will find songs of praise that say: "A man from Baekje named Ye was also a General for China and he died at the age of 58. At the will of the Chinese Emperor, his funeral was conducted with the Chinese Emperor’s honor. When he swung his sword it was like stars in the sky and when he pulled his arrow it was like a full moon.” How do you think a general from Baekje was able to hold such a high ranking position in China and be praised even after his death[Narration]It was July 13, 660 A.D., and an alliance between Silla, a kingdom located in the southeastern region of the Korean Peninsula, and China had conquered Baekje’s capital. However, King Uija, the last ruler of Baekje, had miraculously escaped and succeeded in mounting a second defensive campaign in Gongju. He blocked off all the food supply routes to the Silla-China allied troops, and thus, the war situation had changed and Baekje had time on its side. However,[Soldier 1]General Ye, what is it that you want at this late hour The King is already sleeping…. Aagh!It is treason… [King Uija]Who is it![Ye] Your highness, it is your servant, Ye. Would you serve my knife and die here Or are you willing to surrender to China[King Uija]You stupid fool! Silla and the Chinese are starving to death! Time is on our side, so why are you committing such a terrible crime[Ye]I don’t think you understand me. Here, take the King! [King Uija] Stop! I command that you stop restraining me immediately! I cannot believe this is the collapse of the 700-year-old Baekje Dynasty…[Narration] On July 18, King Uija surrendered to the China-Silla alliance. This was the end of the 700-year-old Baekje kingdom. The last king of Baekje was taken as a prisoner of war to China, while the traitor Ye was rewarded with the highest promotions. The evidence for this story is provided by the rubbing of the tombstone that you see before you.

오디오명: The Heartfelt Dream of Reviving Baekje
대본내용: The artifacts you see now is a stele engraved with an image of approximately one thousand Buddhas. In the center there are two Buddhist saints along with Buddha himself and then on top there are almost 1,000 Buddhists engraved. What did the people desire so ardently that they carved out one thousand Buddhas There are four lines of writing on each side of the central Buddha. The inscription says "In 679 A.D., 250 Baekje people pray for all the people that died with the collapse of Baekje and in the process of their revival movement, including the King of Baekje, his followers, and their ancestors." After the collapse of Baekje in 660 A.D., people from all over the kingdom joined together in a movement to revive the country. They gathered an army with King Uija’s son as their leader and new king. The collapse of Baekje was merely a collapse of the central government, and with the exception of a few regions, the Baekje army spearheaded the revival movement and dominated the territory. They fought ferociously for four years and recovered hundreds of fortresses, but internal conflicts along with the continued oppression by the Silla-China alliance eventually defeated them. Silla's king nominated the former noblemen of Baekje to high-ranking positions in an attempt to gain loyalty. This stele was discovered in a region where the revival movement of Baekje was especially active. The Baekje people of this region were not moved by Silla’s efforts at conciliation and made this stele in remembrance of their efforts to revive their country.

스팟명: Seated Buddha of Seohyeol Temple Site
오디오명: Seated Buddha of Seohyeol Temple Site
대본내용: [Narration] Please take a look at this Sakyamuni Buddha statue.[Tourist]This statue is made of stone.[Narration] Yes. In addition to the gilt-bronze statues that you saw earlier, many stone statues of Buddha was also created in many regions of the Korean Peninsula.[Tourist]Ah, I see. But why is there a hole in between the eyebrows of this Buddha statue [Narration] This represents the white hairs that were said to grow between the eyebrows of Buddha to represent the unlimited powers of the Buddha. Sometimes jewels such as crystals were placed here. I believe that the hole we see was where a crystal had been placed. [Tourist] Oh, I see. I guess that Sakyamuni was able to shoot lasers from between his eyebrows. [Narration]Maybe not so much. Sakyamuni ordered people not to make statues of him. He objected to the idea that people should serve him as if he were a saint by worshipping him. That's why for 500 years after his death, there were no statues that resembled the Sakyamuni Buddha. However, as Alexander the Great entered India, Greek culture was introduced to Asian region. In Greece, it was common for mythical gods to resemble men. As this culture was introduced to India in and around the 1st century A.D., figures of Buddha began to appear. Furthermore, as belief in Buddhism began to wane, Buddhist leaders decided that statues of Buddhist saints were necessary. The statues were designed to be easily distinguishable from common people by their appearance. The Buddhas were made with white hair in between the eyebrows, a feature that symbolized wisdom and divine power. [Tourist] I see. But, I also notice that the facial features of the statue are different from the facial features of the gilt-bronze statue that we saw before. Its face is not as chubby, and the statue's expression is more serious. [Narration]You are right. That was a good evaluation. This Buddhist figure was not made during the Baekje Dynasty. It was made after Baekje was incorporated into Silla, the kingdom occupying the southeastern part of the Korean Peninsula. The characteristics of a Baekje sculpture, such as chubby cheeks and a gentle smile, disappeared during this time. This Buddhist sculpture follows the traditional form of a Silla Buddhist statue.

스팟명: Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty 1
오디오명: An Encounter with the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty, UNESCO World Heritage Sites
대본내용: Welcome to the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty.A collection of these royal tombs is regarded as an embodiment of 500-year history of Joseon, the last dynasty of Korea. So, you will be able to learn about the tradition and basic principles of the dynasty from these tombs.Joseon was founded in 1392 and ruled by a succession of 27 monarchs from King Taejo to King Sunjong. The dynasty left a total of 42 royal tombs scattered over 18 locations in the Korean Peninsula. Among them, 40 royal tombs located in South Korea are registered on the list of UNESCO’s World Heritage Sites since 2009.The Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty tend to be more highly regarded historically and culturally than the similar structures in other countries. Royal tombs of all ruling kings and their queens are included and the tombs have maintained their original conditions of architecture and landscape. Also, the annual ancestral rites are well preserved to honor the memory of deceased kings and to show respect for their achievements. Both the tombs and the ancestral rites are often cited as tangible evidence of the tradition, history and aesthetic creativity of the Joseon Dynasty.What makes these cultural legacies even more valuable is the philosophy embedded within them. Joseon kings wished their graves to represent the austere and dignified life of their ancestors and to be simple rather than grand, so that their construction would reduce the burden upon their people. They were extremely careful in paying respect to their ancestors through traditional rituals and the meticulous management, and sought to build sanctuaries in harmony with nature, thereby inflicting little damage on the surrounding environment.That explains why the tombs still maintain great harmony with the surrounding natural environment even though they are now located in urban areas. Today, a variety of structures in the graveyards are comfortably embraced by the natural environment, and many of these royal tombs are regarded as historic parks.Now, let’s start an exciting tour of these valuable heritage sites and enjoy a leisurely walk through the attractive greenery. You will also have an opportunity to learn some interesting aspects of the Joseon Dynasty.

스팟명: Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty 2
오디오명: The Construction of the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty
대본내용: The Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty were planned and built on the basis of Confucianism and the principles of feng shui. According to feng shui theory, the energy of natural surroundings, such as mountains and bodies of water, was believed to affect one’s life and fortune. Therefore, it was crucial to find an auspicious site for building houses or graves. Especially, the burial site of a king or queen was regarded as being particularly important because it would be directly related with the state's destiny. As for the royal tomb, they preferred a serene and snug place located within about forty kilometers of the royal palace. Spots of outstanding natural beauty were chosen for royal tombs, and they have their back protected by mountains and face wide open space at the front.However, it was not just feng shui that played a crucial role in preparing the royal tomb. The Confucian society put a high value on filial piety. Finding the best place for their deceased parents was also regarded as an expression of the filial piety.Of course, the construction and management of these tombs was one of the most important duties for the people, even in today's Korea.Therefore, the construction of a tomb for a dead king or queen was a very important state affair, and each of Joseon’s kings was obliged to complete it successfully.

스팟명: Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty 3
오디오명: Understanding the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty
대본내용: The Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty exhibit interesting similarities in terms of their layout, buildings and monuments. However, there are some variations according to the period they were built. Such differences can be found in the details of their stone monuments, figures, shrine buildings, and grave mounds. These differences are due to the decisions of the kings who actively participated in the construction, offering their own views and intentions. Here are some interesting facts you should know before you visit the royal tombs.First, the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty are classified into several different types according to the features of their burial mounds. Some tombs have separate burial mounds for the king and his queen, while others have a single mound and they were buried together.Second, in accordance with the ritual decorum of Confucianism, people of higher status were buried west of those of lower rank when they shared the same burial ground. That explains why the king’s burial mound is typically located west of the queen’s. If a king had two legitimate wives, his first consort was buried next to him, and the second was buried next to the first queen, farther away from the king.Finally, each of the royal tombs had its own name, and the names were given according to the virtues or achievements of the king or queen. These virtues and achievements were also reflected in the posthumous honorary titles conferred upon the deceased royals.

스팟명: Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty 4
오디오명: A Road to a Royal Tomb of the Joseon Dynasty
대본내용: Each of Joseon’s royal tombs consists of three sections.The first section of the tomb precincts covers the space from the outer entrance to Hongsalmun, or Red Arrow Gate. It marks the boundary between the two realms, the sacred and the profane.The section contains a passageway leading to the burial mounds, and this passageway gently winds itself to preserve the burial mounds from the visitor’s view. The gate standing at its end was painted red, because the color was believed to help repel evil forces from the sacred burial grounds.The second section is where annual ancestral rites are held. Here at the gate begins the two-lane path leading to the tomb’s shrine called Jeongjagak. The raised lane was intended for the officiator of the ancestral rites, while the lower lane was for the king who was the chief mourner at such rites. There are two buildings on both sides of the lane. One is a kitchen where offerings were prepared, and the other is a guards’ house. The path ends at the shrine, where the ancestral rites took place.The third section is located at the innermost, loftiest part of a royal tomb and is marked by either one or two burial mounds. Each mound is typically protected by stone statues of guards, court officials and animals, and furnished by a variety of stone objects used in the ancestral rites held here.

스팟명: Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty 5
오디오명: Stone Guards and Structures around the Burial Mounds
대본내용: Burial section of the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty houses a variety of stone sculptures. They were designed to decorate these tombs just like the royal palace they had lived.The most conspicuous feature of the burial section is the mound containing the remains of the king and his queen. The mound is enclosed by a set of stone railings and is further protected by a stone screen built on three sides. It is also guarded by stone statues of animals, such as rams and tigers, placed around the mound just inside the screen. Also, stone statues of court officials, both civil and military, stand immediately in front of the mound with their horses to serve their king and queen. There is a rectangular stone table where the ancestral spirits rest, a stone lantern emitting “the light dispelling evil spirits”, and a pair of memorial posts as well.

스팟명: Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty 6
오디오명: Ancestral Rites Held at the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty
대본내용: The rites are still held every year by the descendants of the royal family, and they follow the same procedures established over 600 years ago. The rites are another important factor that registered the Royal Tombs in the UNESCO World Heritage list.The Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty enshrine the bodies of the kings and their queen consorts while the Jongmyo Shrine houses their mortuary tablets. In the Joseon Period, the rites in honor of the virtues and achievements of the royal ancestors were held at both these royal tombs and the Jongmyo Shrine. Before making a formal visit to the royal tomb, the king cleansed his mind and body. Usually, the king’s visit was conducted as a ceremonial procession starting from the palace. As the king needed to show himself in person at the rite, all the royal tombs had to be located in the vicinity of the capital, today’s Seoul. After arriving at the T-shaped Wooden Shrine, the king burnt incense, offered wine, and recited prayers to his ancestors.These tombs represent the Korea’s special cultural legacy, such as loyalty and filial piety, and the core tradition of holding ancestral rites. In other words, the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty can be regarded as a perfect ensemble that resonates with the basic principles of the Confucianism and its aesthetic sense. From now, let’s visit to the individual royal tombs and meet the rich dramas of the 500-year history of the Joseon Dynasty.

오디오명: Donggureung, the Largest Royal Cemetery of the Joseon Dynasty
대본내용: Donggureung literally means the “Nine Tombs of the East”. This royal cemetery was named so because they are located in the east of Seoul, today’s City of Guri.Joseon Dynasty was built on the basis of Confucian ideology and highly regarded virtues such as loyalty, filial piety and humility. Naturally, the ideology affected all aspects of the Joseon Dynasty including the establishment and management of the royal tombs. As humble Confucian followers, the Joseon kings wanted the tombs of their ancestors to be simple and modest. However, the royal ancestors were regarded as parents of all their people and symbols of the state itself. So, the royal tombs still should be solemn enough to pay maximum respect to them. That is why the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty feature comfortably small sizes, giving little burden to the surrounding nature and creating a peaceful harmony with it. Of course, the nine royal tombs in this royal cemetery are no exceptions. Especially, Donggureung tends to attract more attention than others, because it includes the Royal Tomb of King Taejo, the founder and the first king of the dynasty. This huge royal cemetery was established as his successors built royal tombs near this area for more than 400 years. Now then, let’s move and meet at each tomb.

오디오명: Crown Prince Hyomyeong and His Consort, Queen Sinjeong
대본내용: Sureung is the royal tomb of Crown Prince Hyomyeong and his consort. He was the eldest son of King Sunjo, the 23rd king of the dynasty, and his consort was posthumously titled Queen Sinjeong. Traditionally, husband and wife were buried under a single mound in the belief that such burial would help the couple continue the marital relationship even after death. And this tomb is a fine example for this tradition. According to historical records, the prince was a devoted filial child and an ambitious young man full of reformative ideas. When he was eighteen, he started active participation in political affairs on behalf of his sick father and continued to make distinguished achievements in the political arena in the 19th century. For instance, he tried hard to restore royal sovereignty by counteracting the political influence of maternal relatives. He also maintained politically impartial stance in recruiting capable resources for the government. Also, he was a talented man of letters and performing arts enthusiast. He wrote many poems and published essay collections. He even wrote lyrics for the musical works and choreographed dances performed in the court.  Unfortunately, the prince died at the age of twenty two before receiving the opportunity to be crowned. Accordingly, his tomb was built as that of a crown prince at first. His tomb was changed later to fit the status of a king, after his son was enthroned to be King Heonjong, the 24th king of the Joseon Dynasty.

Crown Prince Hyomyeong, the occupant of this royal tomb, died young before he was given an opportunity to be crowned. However, his consort relished a long, prosperous life, and she is generally referred to by her posthumous title Queen Sinjeong. History shows that she was a powerful charismatic figure and actively engaged herself in the state and royal household affairs until her death in 1890 at the age of eighty two. After the prince died in 1830, an eight-year-old boy born between the prince and his consort was enthroned. The new king, Heonjong, honored his deceased father with a king’s title, and raised his mother’s royal status to a queen dowager. Sadly, King Heonjong died young, too, leaving no heir to succeed to the throne. It was eventually given to a minor royal who had been leading a poor farmer’s life. The farmer king died only fourteen years after he was crowned, again, leaving no heir. By then, Queen Sinjeong was in the status of the queen grandmother and the most senior member of the royal family. It meant that she had the right to appoint the heir to the crown.After much consideration, she adopted the twelve-year-old son of a distant relative of the royal family, and crowned him into the 26th king of the dynasty. She expected the alliance with the boy king’s biological father to help her remove her political rivals and move ahead with various reform plans. In the following ten years, the two regents drove out corrupted officials and reformed the tax system. Also, they decided to abolish private Confucian academies because their political influence had been a lot of threats to royal sovereignty. Crown Prince Hyomyeong’s untimely early death deprived him of the opportunity to be king and rule the country as he had planned. Ironically, however, the opportunity was given to his consort. While relishing a long life, she had a large influence in the history of Joseon during the late 19th century.

오디오명: A Tragic Chapter in the History of the Joseon’s Royal Family
대본내용: Hyeolleung is the royal tomb of King Munjong, the 5th king of the Joseon Dynasty. He is buried with his consort, Queen Hyeondeok, under separate mounds. The mound in the west is the king’s and that in the east the queen’s.He was the first child of King Sejong, the 4th king of the dynasty who is generally regarded as the greatest king in Korean history. Historic records say that King Munjong was a devoted filial son and a man of great knowledge. Even when he was a crown prince, he played a very important role in the political arena and actively helped his father ruling the country. Unfortunately, he suffered from ill health for most of his life, and abruptly died just two years after being crowned. Misfortune had attacked his consort, before it did her husband. Queen Hyeondeok, then crown princess, died just three days after giving birth to a boy. This boy later became King Danjong, and he was also the subject of one of the most tragic events in the history of the dynasty.King Danjong inherited crown at the age of twelve. However, his uncle Suyang decided to take the crown from his young nephew. His excuse was that the newly founded dynasty needed a powerful ruler to be protected from ambitious aristocrats. The usurpation of the throne was followed by the boy king’s tragic death and the disgrace upon his mother. However, Suyang had to face the tragic destiny as well. After he was enthroned, he suffered from skin disease for a long time. Moreover, his eldest prince died at the age of 20, in the same year when King Danjong died, and the second son also died only 13 months after the enthronement.

오디오명: King Seonjo who Endured the Darkest Period of the Joseon Dynasty
대본내용: The occupant of Mongneung is King Seonjo, who ruled the dynasty during the late 16th century. He came to the throne at the age of sixteen, following the untimely death of his predecessor, King Myeongjong. As he was young and born to a minor royal, the teenage king tried hard to establish his own power base by recruiting new, talented officials. Actually, some of the greatest historic figures in Korean history were produced during this period.Unfortunately, his reign was also one of the most challenging, turbulent years in the entire history of the dynasty. The hard times began in 1592 when several tens of thousands of Japanese troops arrived at the southeastern part of Joseon. It was the start of the seven year war. The formidable invaders were armed with advanced weapons like matchlock guns, and the miserably unprepared land troops of Joseon lost almost all the early battles. However, the Japanese invasion ended in failure, owing to the rise of civilian fighters and the support from the Ming Dynasty of China. More than anything else, Admiral Yi Sun-sin led the impossible victories in all the battles fought in the seas.  After the war, King Seonjo tried his utmost to rebuild the devastated country and boost people’s morale. He restored government documents burnt during the war, and awarded prizes to people who accomplished distinguished achievements.

오디오명: King Taejo, the Founder of the Joseon Dynasty
대본내용: Geonwolleung is the royal tomb of King Taejo, the founder and the first ruler of the Joseon Dynasty. That explains why the tomb is located at the most elevated part of this royal cemetery.Before founding his own dynasty in 1392, he was one of the most capable general of the Goryeo Dynasty. During the 14th century, the rulers of Goryeo were suffering from a lack of royal authority and political intervention from the Mongolian Yuan Dynasty. Corrupted aristocrats and Buddhist monks were also threats to the royal court. In addition, military conflicts with neighboring countries made people’s life harder. During this turbulent period, he won numerous battles and rose into prominence. His distinguished service as a military leader helped him earn the people’s favor and supports from the emerging Neo-Confucian elites. He exploited the favorable condition to overthrow the old, already crumbling, dynasty and set up his own kingdom.He chose Joseon for the name of his new kingdom and Seoul as its capital. While he was an excellent warrior, he was also a competent politician. He actively invited ideas of great Confucian thinkers of his time as a firm spiritual base for the new kingdom. He established a foundation for the government based on the rule of law for the first time in Korean history, and reformed the land system to increase agricultural productivity.King Taejo’s achievement can be summarized by the establishment of a country governed by Confucian ideology and the development of agriculture to boost people’s welfare. These two, among others, helped form the identity of Joseon.

오디오명: How the King Taejo’s Dying Wish was Ignored
대본내용: King Taejo had two wives, but he is buried alone in this tomb. Where are his wives, thenHis first wife died in 1391, one year before he founded the new dynasty. She was buried at a site in today’s Gaeseong in North Korea. He loved his second wife, Queen Sindeok, so dearly. Using his affection, she persuaded him to hand over the throne to her son, not to his first wife’s son. Also, when she died before him, the old king was left in total devastation. So, he broke the regulation and built her grave within the Seoul City Wall, and even constructed a Buddhist temple to commemorate her.However, two years later, a tragic incident happened and it changed the destiny of Joseon. King Taejo’s son from the first wife, resented his stepbrother’s succession to the throne, and struck the palace and killed the king’s supporters including the crown prince. After this incident, the founder of the Joseon Dynasty lost his power. Moreover, when he planned to be buried beside his second wife, the new king never allowed his father’s earnest intention. Finding that his plan would be infeasible, the founder king asked his son to build his burial mound planted with pampass grass brought from his hometown in the dynasty’s northernmost province. The new king found the most auspicious burial site in the capital, and buried his father here. Then, he had the burial mound of his father’s tomb planted with pampass grass brought from his father’s hometown. It must have been an act of atonement for his unfilial conduct. If you visit here in autumn or winter, you can see the mound is covered with pampass grass while the rest of the royal tombs are covered with turf. King Taejo was a great war hero and the founder of a dynasty which existed on the Korean Peninsula for over five hundred years. The last phase of his life was, however, infused with a sad story of a man who lost loved ones because of the political struggle.

Hwireung is the royal tomb of Queen Jangnyeol. She was the second wife of King Injo, who ruled the dynasty during the mid-17th century. Records tell us that she experienced six state funerals during her lifetime. Some of these funerals led to extremely fierce controversies over the type of mourning garments the queen should wear and how long.The Joseon Dynasty was built on the Confucian ideology, and it had extremely strict rules about funerals. For instance, the rules of the state funeral provided details about who wear what type of garments for how long. In short, the protocol meant the status of the deceased and the mourner’s relationship with them as well as the royal authority. The controversies over her mourning garments were not only developed into a matter of the conventional formalities. It also intensified the strife between political factions and disrupted the state administration. In the end, the mourning garment controversies reflected the vulnerability of the royal family. King Injo had removed his predecessor from power via a coup to take the throne. So, this left the new king’s family vulnerable to the influence of powerful aristocrats. The conflicts over the queen’s mourning garments were dramatic examples to show the political situation at that time.

오디오명: King Yeongjo, Unfolding the Golden Days of Joseon
대본내용: Wolleung is the royal tomb of King Yeongjo, the dynasty’s 21st ruler, and his second Queen. Along with his grandson, King Jeongjo, he is widely regarded as a wise and capable ruler who opened up an era of unprecedented social stability and cultural achievement. He was born to a court lady of low rank, but grew to the longest living and longest reigning monarch of the dynasty. He ruled Joseon for 52 years until his death in 1776 at the age of 83.Once brought to the throne, he started to cure his dynasty’s chronic disease of factional strife in the government. He adopted a policy to recruit talents regardless of what faction they belonged to and contributed to the achievement of political stability. The king was also fond of learning, so he regularly held academic meetings on Confucian classics. Through these meetings, he studied and exchanged views on various state affairs, and made significant achievements in legal and agricultural policies.He was faithful to the teachings of Confucianism and led a frugal life. He focused all his attention on people’s safety and welfare as well. For example, he reformed tax system to reduce the burden of taxes on people, and prohibited harsh corporal punishment. His achievements include the anti-flood project for Cheonggyecheon stream flowing across the heart of Seoul. This project also created many jobs for the poor families, and boosted the economy in the area.His people-centered governance and love of knowledge were transferred to his successor, and the dynasty’s cultural achievements reached a zenith. Take a look around the two burial mounds of the royal couple. There is no stone screen behind them, and the stone railings enclosing them are marked by delightfully plain carving. They still reflect the king’s austere lifestyle.

오디오명: Cemetery for the King Sukjong’s Family
대본내용: Seooreung literally means the “Five Tombs of the West”. As it suggests, this royal cemetery is located at a site in the west of the royal palace. It is the second largest royal cemetery following Donggureung.As you may have noticed already, it’s easily accessible from downtown Seoul. That is why it tends to attract more visitors than other royal tombs of the Joseon Dynasty. Visitors in this royal cemetery can get to another royal cemetery located nearby, called Seosamneung.This royal cemetery began to be created in 1457, when a tomb was built to bury the first son of King Sejo. Today, the cemetery contains the tombs occupied by King Sejo’s two sons and their consorts.Also, it can be regarded as the family cemetery of King Sukjong, the 19th king of Joseon. His three queens, daughter-in-law and king himself are in this cemetery. The cemetery also contains another woman of King Sukjong, Royal Lady Jang. She was one of the most famous royal concubines and had once been in the status of Queen.Except for the tomb of King Sukjong, all the rest of the tombs in this cemetery are only partially open to the public for the protection of valuable historical heritage.Now, let’s start our tour to the graves of Joseon kings and their queen consorts in this cemetery.

오디오명: Queen Died Young from Small Pox
대본내용: Ingneung is the royal tomb occupied by Queen Ingyeong. She was the first wife of King Sukjong, but she is buried alone although her husband is also buried in the same royal cemetery. What happened to this royal coupleBorn to a powerful aristocratic family, she was selected to marry the crown prince when she was only eleven years old. Two years later, she became queen when her husband was enthroned in 1674. However, she died from small pox at the age of twenty, just ten years after she was brought to the palace.Just as in the West until the eighteenth century, small pox had often been a fatal disease for many people, and she was one of the unfortunate victims. Once she was found to have been caught by the horrible disease, she had to be isolated even from her husband until the very final moment.While she was the king’s first wife, little is known about the love story between the royal couple. Sadly for her, King Sukjong didn’t seem to insist that he would be buried with his first wife. He is buried with the two wives he married after her.

Gyeongneung contains two burial mounds built for Crown Prince Uigyeong and his wife. The prince’s wife is now known with her posthumous title Queen Sohye. According to the Confucian tradition, a husband’s mound should be west of his wife’s. However, in Gyeongneung, the prince’s grave is located east of his wife’s. More interestingly, the queen’s grave appears far grander than her husband’s. You must be curious why.The crown prince died at the age of twenty. It’s typical that crown prince’s grave must be plain compared with royal tombs for his senior family members. Contrastingly, his wife’s grave is much grander with more elaborately carved monumental stones. This is because her status was the queen grandmother at the time of her death.Records show that she was a woman of literary talent, political vision and iron will to attain her vision. She wrote a book on the instructions for women to deal with the duties of royal and aristocratic ladies. She was also one of the most influential political figures of her time, and lived a turbulent life full of ups and downs.She witnessed her father-in-law taking the crown from his young nephew, and exploited her political skills to make her fatherless son succeed his uncle and become the king. Her son’s enthronement gave her the title of queen mother along with enormous political power. However, her glorious life ended abruptly and tragically when she had to confront the misrule of her violent grandson.

Hongneung is a grave of a queen who was destined to wait for her husband who would never come back to her. The heroine of this sad story is Queen Jeongseong, the first wife of King Yeongjo who ruled the dynasty during the mid-18th century. She married him when she was just thirteen years old. She shared many great moments in his life, when he was invested as the crown prince, and then enthroned. Later, the king wrote about the 43 years he spent with his first wife, and appreciated her filial piety.Actually, the king wished to be buried next to his devoted first wife at his death, and had a plot for his own grave to the right of the queen’s grave. That is why the queen’s burial mound appears a little lopsided in the tomb’s burial section. However, his wish was never realized. King Jeongjo, his grandson and successor to the throne, wanted a more auspicious site for the grandfather and the dynasty. Also, the new king had to think about his grandfather’s second wife who was alive at that time. In the end, the deceased king was buried in Wolleung, sharing it with his second wife.

Changneung is the royal tomb of King Yejong and his wife Queen Ansun. The king was born as the second son to King Sejo, and succeeded to the throne after his brother who died young. However, he died just fourteen months after being crowned, at the age of nineteen.The young king reigned for such a short period, but some of the most significant achievements were made under his ruling. Especially, through his achievement in land reform, farming lands were distributed to poor farmers so that they could relieve themselves from poverty.However, his tomb site was not regarded as auspicious. Feng shui experts even in his period warned that using the site would end with the death of important members of the royal family.Was it because of the inauspicious site His successor was not his offspring but the son of his elder brother who had also died young before him. All of the king’s children, two sons and a daughter, died without children.This tomb has also suffered many fire accidents, and often dubbed as the tomb of ordeals.

오디오명: Tomb for a Heroic Fighter against Powerful Political Factions
대본내용: Myeongneung is the royal tomb of King Sukjong and his two wives.He is renowned for the efforts he made to break a political deadlock during his reign. The government of the Joseon Dynasty was organized and run by officials. They belonged to several different political factions, and the system was similar to today’s political parties. To strengthen royal authority and maintain balance in his government, he made many transitions of major policies and key positions between the political factions. Such reformation of the government often involved dramatic changes in the lives of his queens and concubines. As they were almost always connected with the major political factions, their destiny tended to be affected by the political situations. Therefore, the rise and fall of the royal ladies had a direct impact on the factions supporting them, and vice versa. Among the king’s several wives, Queen Inhyeon and Royal Lady Jang had particularly dramatic lives. Many Korean people were fascinated by the story of these two women, and a number of TV dramas and movies have been made based on the queen’s ordeal and happy ending and Jang’s ambition and tragic death. King Sukjong was an astute ruler armed with political skills. He fought hard with ruling conservatives to expand his tax reform plan to the entire nation. He adopted a new, much practical, monetary system, and greatly contributed to commercial development. He also introduced the identification plaque system for more effective governance, and it can be comparable to today’s national identification system.

오디오명: Graves for the Royals Suffered by Mischievous Destiny
대본내용: Seosamneung literally means the “Three Tombs of the West”. This royal cemetery not only contains three royal tombs, but also includes graves for minor royals and placenta chambers. When royal members were born, the afterbirths were collected and enshrined in these monumental stone chambers. The tradition of the Joseon Dynasty did not allow the graves of royal concubines, princes or princesses in any royal tomb. However, these graves for minor royals and childbirth monuments were gathered here in the middle of confusion during the Japanese colonial rule and the rapid industrialization in the 20th century. It is rather sad to witness such violation of royal traditions and rules in this cemetery. Among the three royal tombs in this cemetery, only two, Huireung and Yereung, are open to the public.

오디오명: A Story of Family Reunion and Separation
대본내용: Huireung is the royal tomb of Queen Janggyeong, the second wife of King Jungjong who ruled the Joseon Dynasty during the early 16th century. She died of a post-natal illness at the age of twenty five. Originally, her grave was built at a site near the royal tomb of Heonilleung in the south of Seoul. But the grave had to be moved to the current location, according to a plot by an aristocrat named Kim An-ro. After his son married to a princess of King Jungjong, he insisted that the queen’s grave was on an inauspicious site. He exploited the occasion to remove his political rivals who had been related with the construction of her tomb.Despite the complications, all seemed to end well for her when the king was buried next to her. But there was a dramatic reversal of destiny waiting for her. The king’s powerful new wife lived long after his death, and moved his grave to a new location, leaving the short-lived queen all alone.

오디오명: Resting Place of a Filial Son
대본내용: Hyoreung is the royal tomb of King Injong and his wife Queen Inseong. Unfortunately, this tomb is not open to the public. He was born as the first son to King Jungjong. He became a king of the Joseon Dynasty in 1544, but died just 9 months after enthronement. He is widely known and admired for his extraordinary filial piety. His last wish was that he should be buried beside his parents. That explains why his tomb is named Hyoreung which literally means the “Royal Tomb of Filial Piety”.His wish came true as he was buried in this royal cemetery near the tombs of his parents. Sadly, his father’s grave was moved from the cemetery to a new location by his stepmother. Her excuse was that her husband’s tomb site was not auspicious according to the feng shui principles. But everyone knew that the queen’s intention was to separate the king from his another wife and their son.

오디오명: Grave of a Man who Went from Nobody to Somebody
대본내용: Yereung is the royal tomb of King Cheoljong and his queen. This king has a dramatic story about his enthronement. His predecessor, King Heonjong, died without an heir in 1849, and the dead king’s mother moved fast to find the best candidate to be the heir to the throne. Finally, she found a young, uneducated farmer living at a remote village to the west of the capital. Actually, he was a grandson of a royal family member, who was banished as a victim of political strife. For the queen, he was surely closest in line of succession to the throne, and swiftly declared him the heir to the throne. The young farmer became the 25th king of the dynasty at the age of nineteen. However, the dead king’s mother became the queen grandmother, and she ruled the country as his regent. In fact, he was chosen to be king not because he was the most legitimate heir to the throne but because he was a royal with no power behind him. So, the dynasty in this period was under the control of the powerful queen grandmother and her relatives.Gradually, the king came to prepare himself to rule the country with his own plan. He tried hard to make his country to a better place for common people to live in. However, even after the queen grandmother’s regency ended, there was not much for him to do. He died at the age of thirty three, just fourteen years after enthronement.

오디오명: Tomb for the Builder of the National Code
대본내용: Seolleung is the royal tomb of King Seongjong and his wife Queen Jeonghyeon. He became king in 1469 when he was just thirteen years old, and reigned for 25 years when he died at the age of thirty eight.He made distinguished achievements in many areas, and systematically established the foundation for the dynasty. Today’s historians agree that the greatest of all he accomplished is the completion and publication of The National Code. As the title suggests, The National Code is a vast, systematic collection of laws to rule the country. It includes all laws, edicts, regulations and customs established for about one hundred years since the foundation of the dynasty. For the king, it was a complete record of the past and a blueprint for the future.Surprisingly, the code shows that the dynasty had modern features in the system of running the country. For instance, the kings operated a highly developed system for the inspection, expostulation and impeachment of government officials. The system was effective to prevent corruption of officials and misuse or abuse of their power.King Seongjong is also admired for the cultural achievements made in various areas. He promoted learning and education, and diverse books on philosophy, history, music and other topics were published during his reign.

오디오명: Grave of a King Sleeping Alone and Restless
대본내용: Jeongneung is the royal tomb of King Jungjong, who took the crown via a coup in 1506. His predecessor, King Yeonsangun, turned into a hideous despot after he found that his biological mother was hated by the royal family and punished to death. Therefore, King Jungjong’s coup could receive widespread support not only from the ruling class but from common people as well. Following the success of the coup, the new king committed to consolidating the royal authority and stabilizing his government. He tried to rejuvenate the Neo-Confucian politics and promoted education. He also reinstated victims of political strives and used resources to improve defense capabilities. The king had three queen consorts, but is buried alone in this tomb. He couldn’t be with his first queen because she was from the family directly related with his disgraced predecessor. So, he was once buried next to his second queen who had died before him. But his tomb was moved to this place, because his third queen didn’t want her husband to sleep with her rival. Meanwhile, the king’s third queen had to give up her plan to be buried next to her husband because the site was regularly flooded. So, her grave was also built alone in another place. That explains how King Jungjong was separated from all his three wives and buried here alone.

오디오명: A King who Became Cold-hearted for His Dynasty
대본내용: Heolleung is the royal tomb of King Taejong and his wife Queen Wongyeong. He was born as the fifth son to the founder of the Joseon Dynasty, and he played a crucial role in the process of revolution led by his father. However, after the new dynasty was established, he was excluded from the process of distributing honors. Instead, other participants in the revolution feared his will and capability. And they were right because the prince pulled off a coup and removed all the known political opponents. The success of the coup brought him to the throne, and he gained all the power. He could become king right away if he wanted to, but he insisted that his elder brother should succeed his father. Later, he became the third king of the dynasty. He purged all his political rivals to eliminate threats to the construction of a new kingdom, and his wife’s family was not an exception. In fact, his cold judgments and decisiveness helped the newly founded dynasty fast recover stability and authority.He also adopted the administrative system of six ministries and divided the country by eight provinces. This system became the basis of the administrative divisions of Korea today. It was also during his reign that the dynasty began to make official historic records. It is called The Annals of the Joseon Dynasty, and is now registered in UNESCO’s Memory of the World in 1997. The systems and institutions established during his reign became a firm basis of the dramatic development during the reign of his son, King Sejong.

오디오명: Tomb for the Victim of the Struggle with Powerful In-laws
대본내용: Illeung is the royal tomb of King Sunjo and his wife Queen Sunwon.He was the only surviving son of King Jeongjo. His birth was celebrated nationwide, and he was crowned at the age of eleven after his father’s abrupt death. His enthronement aroused a lot of hopes and expectations among people. However, there was not much he could do because most of the key government posts were in the hands of his wife’s family. As a desperate effort to create a balance of power, he married his heir to a girl from another powerful family. Also, he actively employed the relatives of his daughter-in-law.While the government was still largely controlled by the queen’s relatives, the king could bring stability and balance to his government with his queen’s support. He was particularly interested in people’s lives. He adopted relief programs to support poor families in the years of bad harvest and reduce tax burdens.Personally, the queen had to witness the untimely death of her first son and her grandson. As her grandson died without an heir, the queen, now the queen grandmother, had a right to appoint the successor. So, she found an unknown minor royal leading a farmer’s life at a remote village, and appointed him as her grandson’s heir. Today, she is generally regarded as one of the most charismatic royal ladies of the dynasty. Indeed, she was an unprecedented powerful figure who acted as a regent for two young kings during the 19th century.

오디오명: Tomb for a Vicious Usurper who Turned into a Great King
대본내용: Gwangneung contains two burial mounds, one for King Sejo and the other for his wife Queen Jeonghui.King Sejo is a controversial monarch, who ruled the Joseon Dynasty during the mid-15th century. For one thing, he snatched crown from his young nephew, banished him to a remote place and made him died there. He also killed many loyal ministers who censured his disgraceful deeds. On the other hand, he made remarkable achievements in many aspects of state affairs. So, he is widely admired as a true successor to the great monarch King Sejong.Today, historians seem to focus more on the achievements he made than his misdeed. It is generally agreed that it was King Sejo who laid a firm foundation for the newly established dynasty. To strengthen royal authority, he fought hard against powerful and ambitious aristocrats in and around the royal court. Thanks to correct population survey and land reform, his state treasury with tax revenue grew dramatically. He also reformed the military as an effort to strengthen national defense against foreign enemies. Moreover, it was also under his rule that Joseon established a government agency responsible for the grain price management. For the price stability, the agency bought grains from farmers in the years of good harvest and sold them back in bad years.He had long been blamed for his choice of extreme actions for his ambition and stability of royal authority. But his outstanding achievements gave him a special status, and his spirit tablet is enshrined for good in the royal ancestral shrine.

오디오명: Gwangneung, a Historic Turn in the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty
대본내용: The royal tomb of King Sejo tends to attract more attention, because it has a special position in the history of the Royal Tombs of the Joseon Dynasty. As a devoted Confucian follower, he tried to lead a frugal life. He also left a will to his descendants that they should make his tomb simple and humble so that his people would not suffer much from its building. With the simpler tomb, he wished to save people from hard labor and preserve state funds. His decision influenced the royal descendants to continue to reform and change their burial system. That is why these two burial mounds have only stone railings and no stone screen encircling their lower parts. The tomb also has no paved passage linking the entrance to the T-shaped Wooden Shrine. Still, the vicinity of this tomb has great scenery. The Gwangneung Forest containing the tomb and the Korea National Arboretum was inscribed to the UNESCO World Network of Biosphere Reserves in 2010. You can here relish a peaceful walk around one of the most valuable and best protected green areas in Korea.

The name Yunggeolleung refers to the two royal tombs, Yungneung and Geolleung. Crown Prince Sado and his son King Jeongjo are buried in these tombs with their consorts. Most visitors in these royal tombs are attracted by the tragic story of the crown prince and his devoted filial son.  Crown Prince Sado buried in Yungneung was born as the second son of King Yeongjo. The prince’s death is cited as the most tragic of all the deaths recorded in the history of the Joseon’s royal family. In 1762, the king ordered his crown prince to be sealed in a wooden rice chest and made him died. The prince’s ten-year-old son kept appealing to his grandfather in tears to save his father but it was futile. Crown Prince Sado died of hunger in the rice chest eight days after the confinement.Later, the prince’s son became King Jeongjo, who was a great king of the late 18th century. He tried to recover his father’s honor, and it was one of the most important tasks to fulfill in his life. Indeed, as soon as he came to the throne, he moved his father’s tomb to the current location. He also planned to make a new city. For King Jeongjo, all these efforts were not just to revere the memory of his father but to recover his original status and honor.It was only in the late 19th century that the crown prince was given a posthumous kingly title. Accordingly, the status of his tomb was elevated to a royal grave as well.The prince’s consort, Queen Hyegyeonggung, wrote about her husband’s tragic death and her ordeal when she was sixty years old. She is now happily buried next to her husband in the tomb.

오디오명: Controversy on Death of Crown Prince Sado
대본내용: There are two confronting explanations about the tragic death of Crown Prince Sado. One is that the prince was killed just because he was crazy. He was an intelligent prince when he was young. But, as he grew up, he couldn’t meet his father’s expectation and showed some signs of mental illness from stress. Eventually, he developed mental problems and his father could not accept his strange actions.Others argue that he was a victim of the political struggles. It was the time of extreme power struggle of political factions. He tried to reform his father’s government against powerful conservative aristocrats, and this made him a target to be removed for his political opponents. The prince was in the minor political faction. Even his wife, father-in-law and the king was in the opposition party.No one knows exactly what the truth was. Most of records made at that time depicted the crown prince negatively. Moreover, after the prince’s son became king, he ordered the royal secretariats to remove the official daily records of one year about his father’s death.The ruling political faction continued to show their hostility towards the throne. As far as they were concerned, the prince’s son was the traitor’s offspring, so they raised voices to the new crown prince from enthronement. After he was enthroned, he invested his disgraced father with a posthumous kingly title. He wanted to recover his father’s honor and strengthen the legitimacy of his position. Ironically, his father’s tragic life helped the new king devote all his energy to his fight against powerful aristocrats and for the unprecedented reformation of the dynasty.

오디오명: Tomb of a Ruler Devoted to State Reform
대본내용: Geolleung is the royal tomb of King Jeongjo and his consort Queen Hyoui. He ruled the dynasty during the late 18th century, and is generally regarded as one of the most successful rulers. He is widely admired as a reformer and pragmatist, and made distinguished achievements in many areas. He established the royal library called Gyujanggak to provide ample opportunities for talented scholars regardless of their social status. The library played a crucial role in the accumulation of knowledge and publication of books on various subjects including law and agriculture. Also, he was a good archer himself and keenly interested in military affairs. He encouraged the compilation and publication of books on military strategies, too. Especially, he tried to be extremely careful regarding the imposition of the military service duty. It contributed to the stabilization of the country on the basis of strong supports from people. But his path to the throne had been tough, largely because of the hostility of the powerful aristocrats. That is why the king felt he needed a major reform for the dynasty. Unfortunately, he died in 1800 at the age of forty nine, and all his reform programs abruptly ended. His mysterious death is one of historic accidents modern Korean people feel most sorry about. His tomb was built at the current location according to his wish to stay nearer to his parents even after death.His wife is buried beside him in this tomb. She was a devoted filial lady who took good care of her mother-in-law Queen Hyegyeonggung. She was also widely admired for the frugal and simple life.

오디오명: King Jeongjo’s Historic Royal Procession to Hwaseong
대본내용: During his 24-year reign, King Jeongjo was greatly revered for his devoted filial piety among others. He was always conscious of his duty to his parents as a son. In particular, he moved his father’s grave from Seoul and built a new city including palace and fortress. Especially, Hwaseong Fortress in Suwon is generally accepted as the greatest architectural heritage from Joseon. It was also registered on the UNESCO World Heritage list in 1997.The project should complete fast to avoid the interruption of powerful aristocrats. So, he needed the most advanced and efficient construction methods. It led the dramatic development in the building science and technology of the dynasty, and the 5.5km-long fortress walls with related defense facilities and the royal palace were constructed in just eight months. After the construction of the city, the king wanted to display his firm will to protect royal sovereignty. So, he decided to hold a large state event at the new city, to celebrate the sixtieth birthday of his mother Queen Hyegyeonggung. This huge formal banquet involved the pageantry of royal processions and carried almost the entire royal family and the government. According to the records, all the celebratory events took eight days and over ten thousand people participated in this banquet. Indeed, it was one of the largest events in the history of Joseon. Remarkably, the details of the entire event were carefully depicted by the court painters on a 15m-long folding screens. It includes the royal procession from Seoul to Hwaseong, and is regarded as a valuable cultural heritage of Korea.

Insa-dong is one of the most popular attractions for international tourists visiting Seoul, because it’s probably the best place in the city to see the combination of traditional and modern Korea. During the Joseon Dynasty, a small village not far from Insa-dong was home to Dohwaseo, a government office in charge of illustrating royal processions and events. Many of the employees of Dohwaseo lived in and around the area.However, after the decline of the Joseon Dynasty the office was closed and the workers lost their jobs. Later, their descendants started selling artworks and even the tools used to draw or paint them on the streets of Insa-dong.Gradually they established a market dealing in antique arts and crafts, and that’s how the area became the center of traditional culture and art. Of all the many streets and alleys of Insa-dong, Ssamziegil is the busiest shopping street. Although it's called a street, the name actually refers to the hallways of a four-story shopping center with over 70 stores. The shopping center is worth a visit because in addition to the stores selling traditional handicrafts and souvenirs, there are also galleries and tea shops. There is a basement floor as well, where you can make your own pottery, Korean paper and ceramic products.Opposite Ssamziegil, you'll find a narrow alley. Walk up the alley a short way, and you’ll come across the Insa-dong Tourist Information Center. Also called the Insa-dong PR Center, the main role of this center is to provide information about local stores and restaurants. Besides providing useful information, the center offers free tea and you can also try on royal costumes or the uniform dress worn by the court ladies of the Joseon Dynasty.Above all, the places not to be missed in Insa-dong are the small, private galleries with unique paintings, stationery shops selling traditional everyday necessities and Korean paper, and antique shops. If you would like to try a delicious cup of traditional Korean tea, you may choose to patronize any one of the traditional tea shops and tea houses along the street. In the streets of Insa-dong, you'll get to enjoy the harmony existing between the traditional and the modern.

首史博物是一可以了解到首都首如何建成、如何展的地方。博物展示着各古代、代文物，可人直地看到首的史程。博物的常展分“朝代的首”、“ 港大帝期的首”、“日本强占期的首”、“高速成的首”四大主。  常展的第一展是“朝代的首”，展了朝建后都到港之前的首。在里可以看到光化的前身六曹街,以及族生活的北村和被作“都城外的”的麻浦、山、往十里貌。“港，大帝期的首”展展了港后着西洋文化入而逐生化的首。在里可以看到口、器不已的首人朴的面貌。在“日本强占期的首”展可以看到1910年被日本强制合后，首市民在展立的同，又不得不接受日本帝主文化的情景。 最后，在“高速成的首”展可以了解到1945年解放后至2002年世界杯期首的化。在里，可以看到大民的首都首在的墟中仍困苦，在逆境中求展，成世界一流城市的程。首史博物最有人的所之一是位于三的“城市模型影像”。“城市模型影像”是首按1/1500小后的精巧模型IT技相合的多媒展。里面在首的每座建筑都行了精巧展，令人感不已。如果首有任何好奇，不妨使用城市模型影像角落里的首系行。系可以用多主行索，了解首地理有大助。首史博物是了解首的最佳口。

首起初不是，而是一道路。大帝的高宗皇帝了近代化而在德正大前修建了太平路、小公路等主要道路，些主要道路交形成的空，便是首的形。此后，每次家出危机，都有多市民聚集到里，祖喊助威。里曾生日本强占期最大的抗日立3.1、取民主主的4.19革命、6月民主化抗等重的史事件。然，里也了多令人喜的史瞬。 2002年日世界杯，有无市民聚集于此，高呼“大民！！”足球加油助威，也正是借人的精神，最以傲人的成，成洲第一世界杯四强的家。想的色人潮遍布首和太平路，成世界目的焦点。2002年世界杯助威活之后，有人提在里修建市民集的。果，政府在2004年除了上的泉，上草坪，建成了在的首。 首一年四季是人潮，充活力。夏天凉爽的地面泉，冬天的滑地，吸引着多家庭和情的到。此外，里常“Hi! Seoul Festival（首文化）”等各活，洋溢着喜。首可是首市民精神生活的力源泉。

高大厦林立，川流不息，里便是首的地光化。 光化由位于景福正光化前的常行大模活所用的向10道的用成。古代起，光化就是政治和文化的象征。建立朝的太祖李成桂在建造景福，王公的建筑勤政殿到正光化之修成一直，以便王可以向外看到百姓。光化的大路立了相于在行政部和司法部的6曹。朝代，光化前面曾是王和百姓通交流的所。但在日本强占期之后，光化及周一不遭受磨。日本帝主了扼的精神文化，在朝的正景福建起了朝督府。以住朝督府野由，光化搬到了景福旁。此后，光化在中木制的上端部分也被火毁，1968年然使用混凝土重新修建，但未能回作殿的威。2010年，光化在原的位置上按原型行了原，光化也重新成充史文化息的空。日本强占期之后，曾行的光化前的道路，在成各文化活的市民空。

오디오명: 游光化, 感受史魅力
대본내용: 一到光化就看到威的李舜臣和面微笑的世宗大王像。李舜臣在朝中期抵抗日侵略，拯救了家。在也巍然屹立在光化，守着的中心。世宗大王造了的文字文。世宗大王是消除百姓不字的困而造文的。 走到世宗大王像后，沿着通往地下的台下去看看。里有念世宗大王一生的展空“世宗大王故事”。“世宗大王故事”展采用最尖端的展技法，向游客介世宗大王自幼年期至上王以后所建立的多。通各多媒工具，男女老少都能自然地了解朝期世宗大王在各域展的功。有理解文的程序、日晷用途以及利用3D机器演奏宗祭的地方，游客可情享受其中的趣。世宗故事展面是一座展忠武公李舜臣故事展。在座展中可以看到李舜臣的生活。4D空可船部的空，忠武公的故事注入了新的活力。此外，船、小船等各游活男女老少均可。光化相的周文化施游客提供了更多趣。可欣大型表演的世宗文化、可了解大民近代史的大民史博物、最大的店保文不吸引着游客的目光。

大民史博物是一座立史博物，在里，可以了解朝末期的港期到在的近代史展程。博物一至五共由4常展和2企展成。一有可直接行的最尖端展施企展。 下面按序介一下博物的常展。位于三的第一展以“大民的萌芽”主，展了朝向外放港口到日本治下得解放段期的史程。 第一展中最特的展品是《3.1立宣言》。 《3.1立宣言》是日本强占期最大模的立3.1立始那天在民面前宣的宣言。通踏地面上的文字，壁上立宣言的原文行重，我依可以感受到的那着感。4的第二展以“奠定大民之基”主。主要介了政府成立的意和的苦痛以及民克服困的史。最后到五，里有第三展和第四展。第三展展出的是被“江奇迹”的象征高速展的各品。在大民煌的成背后，有被迫作出牲的大量民。民在忍受苦痛的同，又了煌的展和民主主展，在里可看到其有的各和影像。第四展中可以看到工化成文化强、育强的程。在史博物可以看到身困境，仍巍然屹立于世界上的大民民。

迎到政念。政念是念最早的局政局而修建的博物兼局。政局的首任局是洪英植。洪英植朝末期的政治家，在考察了日本和美的近代化政制度后，回立了具有近代政制度的政局。但是，政局成立20余天了。是由于希望近代化的政局局洪英植等化派企政，使得政府政局强制。 下面介一下政念的展品。在念的院子里，着去主要事件的票被制成了瓷。屋格的政局部展出着最早的票和所穿的衣服、使用的物品、物品寄地。如想政局作一步了解，可通展休息室配置的摸行。政念起初展，自2012年起也始承少量政。政念的游客推出了各政目。仔察的，大家里有下愿望，一年后投的“希望筒”和用自己的照片制成的“人票”，以及在明信片上好容提交申后，在的信息中行展示后再送的“的信息”等。的信息寄外人也可申，但需要提前交。在政念可以了解人有的充情感的浪漫信件。

오디오명: 了市民的放空-首市政府
대본내용: 迎到首市政府大。前面所看到的古典格的建筑是市政大，后面璃的建筑是新市政大。首市政大建于日本强占期，解放后被用作政府公。目前部改造，1~5被作使用，面向全市民放。在里可以免使用互和索，所需信息。市政大的特之在于市公室。是去首任市公的所，此的游客都可以坐在市位子上行拍照留念。2012年投入使用的首市新市政大造型十分特。入一大，1到7的壁上栽有常藤、茉莉花、薰衣草等6万5千盆植物，人一身大型植物的感。些植物具有化空和室度及度的作用。

仁寺洞是首的外游客最常去的地方之一。因仁寺洞是文化代文化融合展的地方。 朝代，仁寺洞附近有制王出巡或其他活的官署署。在署工作的人也居住在里。但是，到了朝末期，着家政出困，署被撤，生活在附近的人的后代，始到仁寺洞街道上出祖先使用的工具和作品。由此，里始慢慢形成古董、美品街，着的流逝，在已成文化的中心地。仁寺洞最人潮鼎沸的建筑是Ssamjigil。一到四，沿着螺旋形走廊分布着70余家商店。其中有工品店、念品店、廊、茶等，游客在里可得特的。Ssamjigil地下有可自制作陶瓷、、粘土工的施。沿着Ssamjigil面的胡同一直走，看到仁寺洞宣。仁寺洞宣是向游客介仁寺洞美食和商店的地方。在里可以一品免茶点，一穿上朝代王、王妃或大今的衣服拍照留念。此外，仁寺洞有展出性的SOHO廊，出生活用品和的房，出古代美作品的美品商店，些都深深地吸引着游客的目光。 如果想品一下的茶，不妨前往仁寺洞街道旁的茶感受一下淡淡的茶香。仁寺洞是代文化的最佳去。

迎到曹溪寺。曹溪寺是在日本强占期的1910年,由祈求恢民族自尊的僧所建的曹溪宗的代表寺院。沿大路入曹溪寺，首先映入眼的是一柱。所的一柱，其有正的，是由一排柱子形成的，的是方便所有人出寺。此外，据一柱含有在走寺院之前一切世俗洗去，心修佛之意。 入曹溪寺，首先看到的是屹立于大雄殿旁的白松，松原本是色的，但着月的流逝，皮逐呈白色。曹溪寺的白松超500年，被指定第9天然念物，非常珍。白松旁有笑得天漫的天佛。塑造天佛是希望年幼的佛信徒能近佛祖。                                                               曹溪寺的中心是模巨大、的大雄殿。具有大英雄在此之意，指的是供奉迦牟尼佛的地方。大雄殿的部供奉着三尊佛祖。在他的右供奉着朝初期雕刻的木制佛祖。  大雄殿的一面上，供奉着祈求信徒往生的牌位。大雄殿前院有八角十的石塔,里面供奉着由斯里的迦牟尼佛祖的身舍利。座塔在2009年行了重建，用代替以前的日本格的建筑。向舍利塔祈愿的“塔”、了佛祖一生的大雄殿壁、曹溪寺的梵、佛博物等建筑共同曹溪寺打造成最受游客迎的寺之一。

曹溪寺位于首市中心，是游客放松身心,加活的好去。在通音佛之心的梵，每天凌晨4点和傍6点左右，都使用佛器行演奏。伴着演奏的曲,人的心也得安。 走大雄殿可以眼看到僧祈的情景。但由于大雄殿是信徒僧一起祈修行的空，所以勿做出妨碍僧人修行之。去佛用品店一下信徒准的祈用品以及游客准的念品，也是一了解佛文化的方式。如果佛和寺寄宿感趣的，可一下曹溪寺的寺寄宿活。如想了解寺院的所有寺寄宿，在曹溪寺面的寺寄宿部，可以得信息。如果需要安慰或休息，光曹溪寺，里的大24小敞。
里便是塔谷公。在座略幽的城市公塔谷公其是最大的抗日立3.1的起地。塔谷公正被作三一。什叫三一是因塔谷公是抵抗日本的立中模最大的3.1立始的地方。1919年日本强占期，人在塔谷公宣了志着3.1始的《立宣言》。此后，由塔谷公始的立不畏日的,迅速展成全性。因此受或死亡的人不其。以宣《立宣言》的所八角亭中心，有展3.1抵抗日本情景的壁和刻有《立宣言》容的立宣言念塔，中可以感受到人的怒和勇。

塔谷公除3.1立以外，是高和朝代的史。高代，塔谷公所在之有一座名福寺的寺院；朝初期建起了一座名寺的寺院。朝代建造的寺址的痕迹可以通位于八角亭右的寺碑得到。寺碑上着然朝代崇拜儒，但朝的第7代王世祖却在福寺址上建起寺,在寺址上建造了13石塔的由。 寺碑由巨大的形碑座和放在上的碑石以及部的雕刻成。八角亭后面有第2寺址十石塔。石塔的上部雕刻着佛祖的一生及逸事。由于寺址十石塔由易的大理石材料所建造，所以正采用璃行保。曾金碧煌的寺在朝中期着政支援的而成墟，被期荒置。1897年高宗皇帝接受英人布朗的建，在同一位置修建了公。公一始被用作皇室祭祀或音演奏的所，在1920年成在的公。

오디오명: 朝代的物商-六矣廛市廛
대본내용: 座位于9地下的博物是六矣廛博物。是2003年在除破建筑程中偶然的埋在地里的朝代的商店，修建后成在所看到的博物。六矣廛是朝代家公的商店，指的是交一定的金，出、粗布、、等六商品的商店。六矣廛曾位于在地站所在的路十字路口附近。朝代，里分布着2500余家市廛，是一模非常大的商街。 接下介一下博物的部。入博物里面看到上着的六矣廛旗，璃地面下方商店部情景行了原。上有可商店位置的地和能看到商人价价情景的，而可以想到朝代的市。博物最有趣的看点是名避洞的街道。避洞由“避和子的路”得名。云街王居住的殿景福相，王和身高的人常和坐由里。他相遇，身低的人停下脚步，到路在地上行，所以避他而在大路旁修建了避路。  自朝代起形成的避洞然路面窄，但道路旁却有多物美价廉的。在然因城市留下路的一部分，但著名的美食街，凡是生活在首的人，一定要去上一次。

오디오명: 宣告新的一年到的普信梵
대본내용: 普信是1895年由大帝的皇帝高宗所起的名字。普信原的名字是，高宗皇帝什座被的建筑起名普信是因朝代儒作中心理念的价。朝以前起就重仁、、、智、信五价。朝建，太祖根据仁、、、智在西南北修建了仁之、敦、崇、靖四座大。此后，在朝末期充四大之，高宗皇帝加入“信”字重新起了普信之名。朝代，普信梵凌晨4点敲33次，出打城的信，上10点敲28次，出城的信。不如此，在都城生大火也敲，向所有百姓出警。普信的梵是敲新年的, 每年12月31日多人聚在里, 只在0点跨年刻聆33次的,人山人海的景象非凡。人聆着新的一年愿祈福。西方的的模相似，但敲的方式不同。西方的里向外敲，但的却是外向里敲。正是因特的方式，雄又悠，得。不新年第一天，而且1月2日到12月24日，除周一外，普信敲活每天正午都行。通首市政府主申的，在定好的日子里可每天白天12点敲12次的普信敲活。大家也敲一下普信梵。

座名叫水，因下放有量水深的水而得名。朝期曾是石，而在是木。水是世宗大王期的著名科家英雨器同制作的象工具。因有了水，百姓才能提前防洪水泛所的害。 座水是朝第19代王宗平民出身的王妃禧第一次面的所。据有一天，宗祭祖回的路上，好在水附近看到了一名叫玉的平民之女。返回中的宗，因久久忘不了那天在水上看到的美女子，果派人把叫了女。后玉借宗的上了王妃。除此之外，有一有趣的故事。朝代，水附近曾有多上游下的沙子堆成的小山丘。据，多无家可的犯人和乞，在山丘上洞生活，以捕蛇生。朝第21代王英祖了他捕蛇的利。一直到在人把捕蛇的人叫做“棍”，也就是“地洞”的意思。了水，沿着溪川一路前行，看到直云的泉和美的激光所展的美面。走在藏着美故事的水上，情享受高大厦造的美夜景!

在看到的是五水。在朝代，首曾被城郭保。五水原本是在城下方的水，是首城郭流出的溪川水路必由的水，因有五座水所以被五水。生活在首都城的百姓所排放的生活水通五水流出。比起戒森的四大城，五水警戒松懈，所以常有人里逃出。溪川位于盆地形的都城中心，由于山上流下的土和百姓的生活水都要溪川流入江，所以河床迅速升高，河水泛，常生水，使得朝代王多次施疏浚工程。朝的第21代王英祖期立了的官署，施了大模疏通工程。五水下面展示着疏浚工程有的文物，其中包括英祖在疏浚工程中立功的大臣的文章和。  五水一直保存至朝末期，日本强占期，以水影溪川水流由其城一起除，在同一位置修建了穿溪川的混凝土。上了最早的道。1926年，大帝最后一位皇帝宗的葬行列也曾里。

오디오명: 描正祖水原出行的《正祖陵行班次》
대본내용: 幅看也非常高大的壁是《正祖陵行班次》。是世界上最大的陶瓷壁，192米，共使用了4960壁。《班次》最前面的片是朝代首的地《首善全》。《首善全》是朝地理家金正浩于1840年自勘地形后制的非常精的首地，使在的地相比也无太大差。  《正祖陵行班次》描了朝第22代王正祖母惠洪氏花甲宴，拜父思悼世子的陵墓而去水原城的情景。《正祖班次》中一共有1779名行人和779匹，但出巡人到6000余名。《班次》是后看整列的情景，描得非常致，可一目了然地了解所有人的位置。 我仔察一下《班次》。行列最前面是的京畿司在引路。他后面是人和女。接下是正祖的母惠洪氏所坐的子。 惠洪氏的后面着一匹，匹上坐的是正祖。但是，却看不正祖的面孔。因王的面容在朝期被是非常尊的，所以不在中出。王的面孔只能出在肖像中，而且必在王去世后才能公。 《班次》不但描了朝期人的面貌，描了正祖父母的一片孝心，是一幅非常的作品。

通始建于600多年前的朝初期，但近100余年一直被埋在地下，最近才重新得原。仔看一下，其中有泛黑的比年久的古代石和新的白色石。 在梁下方，刻有多案的史悠久的石。些石曾是堆王陵的墓石。什陵墓用的石又被用修建梁是朝建初期家族的王之所留下的痕迹。 朝王朝的第三代王太宗本王世子，但能上。因他的母神德王后无王位承序，自己所生的年幼的子李芳立世子。太宗一之下掉同胞母兄弟李芳登上了王位。怨恨未消的太宗下令把母的陵墓葬到城外，而留下的墓石用修建通。 但是，太宗的用心相反，通后成了一座王出行或家重要外使臣一定要路的梁。正月十五在朝代也是重大日之一，天人在通上玩各民游。白天在上放，上。正月十五天是一年中唯一能整宿游玩的日子，各身的人聚在一起夜。是一游，据如果天，一年之腿不生病，所以曾是大部分人所喜的一游。正月十五天吃野菜和花生、核桃等果。是因有打碎果出的“”鬼怪一俗。我也走一下朝代人健康而走的通。

溪是一位于市中心的休，常有周的上班族外游客光。走看到一根色的螺形柱子。根柱子名《spring》，是美流行家“Claes Oldenburg”和“Coosie Van Bruggen”作的作品。据作此作品的家是的瓷器、服及中秋月中得感，而作出了一作品。 spring部端滴下的水珠沿着地面修建的溪川微模型流入下方的溪川。微模型是按一比一百的比例修建的。溪川在朝建初期，曾是百姓生活密切相的河流。但朝亡后，在日本治期，因河水染重，被用混凝土盖起修成路，解放后，到了70年代，又在里修建了溪高架，把原的溪川盖在了地下。光流逝，2003年，被修建成人工河流，重新回到了首市民身。下行至溪川，在段式瀑布的下面，有多自于朝半(包括北)八道的黑色石，些石象征着成南北分裂前的大民的8地。是溪川的名，含着人期盼祖早日一的想。但令人憾的是，北地的石是采用感和特色其相似的石所代替的。希望能早日成正的北地的石。在八石潭可以投幸硬，据如果投大理石的圈，愿望就。迎大家踊行挑，使失也不要得可惜，因投的硬用做善事。溪每季都有各各的活和典在等待着大家，如果想了解史，感受特，就到里情享受。

洞路因路建有多百年以上的建筑和首市立美、洞等文化施及德所造的浪漫而吸引着大批游客前。但一方面，里含着曲折的近代史。位于洞路周的近代西洋式建筑大部分是朝末期至大帝期所建的。洞建有各列强的公使，在他的保下始入。此后，由宗建立了校。此同，如重明殿等象征着近代史展的建筑也如雨后春般涌出。通目前洞路上留下的建筑，可人感受到于列强之，却仍近代化的强意志。洞路不因浪漫的和悠久的史而名，有一有趣的。相情一旦一起走路便分手。是因在20世80年代，家庭法院便位于洞路上，由于常有婚的夫妻路上走出，所以才出“走洞路的情分手”一。 更有意思的是，曾是家庭法院的建筑在首市立美，成有多情前往的地方。

오디오명: 城市中一道美的景屋村
대본내용: 迎到北村！自朝初期以，就被分北村和南村大部分。以路和溪川界，北叫“北村”，南叫“南村”。作首最具代表性的旅游景点之一，北村无什候都是人潮爆。那，北村什如此受迎 首先，北村保存着1,000余座屋，展出在城市中以略到的之美。大大小小的胡同和古色古香的屋，着北村具魅力的史底。而且，北村有具史意的史迹、文化和民俗史料，被是“城市里的博物”。在里可以各主的展和博物，可以到工房的文化。那，些屋里都住什人因北村君王所在的景福和昌德，而且背倚北岳山，面朝溪川，是水地，所以朝代的名望族和班子弟前里建造家宅，于是逐形成了族聚居域。在北村的候一般都遵循“北村八景”的路，所“北村八景”是北村一景色美、适合取景的8地方。到里，是不是已等不及了好，在就我了解一下北村的魅力！，有一件事情要住！因北村的屋目前仍然有人居住，所以大家不要看屋或在胡同里大喧。在准好了那就出～

北村，有曲曲的小胡同，也有多精彩看点，但是始看起如果有的疑，那不妨先到北村文化中心了解一下。是游客成立的服中心，提供多言的北村介和地，游客准了休息。文化中心原本是朝末期名望族亨基（音）其子所建的房屋。他聘中木匠，模昌德后院演堂的式建造了座屋。此建筑在建筑史上也享有，被指定注文化。制定“注文化”制度的目的在于在近代文化中出具有高保存意的目，行特管理。之后房屋和重建，然在外上生了化，但得益于建筑家保存原形所付出的努力，至今仍洋溢着朝末期屋的。北村的屋分布比密集，而北村文化中心在屋村形成之前就已建成，所以相比敞，且外放，便于游客察朝代屋的部。 北村文化中心大民、茶、工、等精彩的文化座，敞大迎接大家的到。到了北村，一定不要忘了到文化中心串串！

北村旅行中的一大趣就是游“北村八景”，好，那我北村第一景始欣！北村第一景是“石外的昌德全景”。昌德建于1405年，是朝王朝建造的第二座王。路北村文化中心往前走，到一小十字路口后，向右坡方向走上去可到昌德。近坡，昌德就像一座藏着的藏，徐徐展身影。石外，最先映入眼的是正殿仁政殿的面。仁政殿是君王理朝政事的地方。再往前就是奎章和璿源殿了。奎章是中用于藏和究的地方，而璿源殿供奉着代君王的肖像。昌德是各代君王居住最的殿，在1997年被合科文列入世界文化名。朝的各大殿中惟有昌德被指定世界文化，是什 去，君王都希望把殿建得雄，以示王室的力。中的故可以是最具代表性的皇室建筑。朝的第一座殿景福也是如此，整座建筑群左右，秩序井然。而昌德遵循自然地形，高低有致、自然大方，周景交相映，且在朝王中保存得最完好，因此被列入世界文化。 在景福，君王的住和理朝政的地方得太近， 活空比窄，而昌德的庭院相敞，所以受到代君王和王妃的。据除了只注于朝政的世宗大王外，大部分君王更喜昌德。

스팟명: 北村第二景：苑西洞工房路
대본내용: 北村的都是王，据曾是官族群居的地方。里果只有有人居住 位于昌德旁的苑西洞，因地王后苑西而得此名。里居住着世世代代在里打的下官和老百姓，因此北村第二景苑西洞工房路上是一些古老而的屋，周的工房一起展出朴素之美。 位于胡同中的廷食究院，是究朝代廷食的地方，其史意得到可，被指定家重要非物文化。食究院由慧性老成立，是得到朝王室末代房的著名。我在《大今》里看到的于廷料理的面就是在此拍的。然踏了廷食究院，那就了解一下代君王享用的御膳走到胡同的，有一可以想象出年老百姓生活的址，那就是“苑西洞洗衣址”。里昌德石相，一直以都是在此居住的老百姓洗衣服的地方，至今依然流着澈的溪水。

中央高中位于桂洞路坡的，至今已有100多年的史。，座西式石造建筑周的屋形成明的比，十分引人目。据中央高中尤其深受外游客喜，因里是曾靡一的《冬季歌》的外景地。多游客都在校前的念品店流明星的照片。 那校里面的景又如何的大部分高中都是四四方方的代式建筑，而在20世初建成的中央高中却佛外大的校，富有情趣。据在修建的考了多美地的西式建筑。 排于主后的和西然也是西式建筑，但主不同，使用的是。座之有一小小的庭院，配有古老的路、椅和。校里面有一院落，是一道特的景。因完好无地保留着建造的面貌，究20世初受外建筑格的影方面有一定助，和西也被列入了史迹名。 中央高中平日有生在上，不外放。想要中央高中部，需要等到周末。周末的放：每月第1、3、5周六下午1点到6点，第2、4周六、周日及假日上午9点到下午6点。

北村第三景是屋胡同，窄的胡同排列着屋建筑。北村的屋大部分都有人居住，所以无法仔看到部。但是可以票入博物或工房里面，工屋部。 如果想欣五六色的刺工品，可以到洙刺博物；如果想了解民和身符等民俗料，可以到嘉民博物；在琳工房古老的品到底有多漂亮。在北村工，无可加精彩的工。 些工房什都聚集在北村 是因上世60年代后期始的潮使屋逐消失，政府了保文化，80年代始施行屋保存政策，除了屋，世代相的文化也之保留下。首市以政府名置屋，其租工工匠。据，北村的尤适合工房，因此多著名家也慕名而，使一逐形成了工房的聚集地。 如果北村文化中心展的是式房屋的魅力，那在里可以略代式房屋的面貌。30年代，一同建起了多座代式屋，而房屋至今有改，保存着老百姓的生活情景。工房更北村更近一步！ 看完北村第三景，走到胡同的，面有“Donmi店”，在此路，可通往 北村第四景。其“Donmi店”也算是一件史文物，因家店已在里了差不多有70年了。

北村的屋典型的屋相比有一些不同，到底有什不同之我了解一下 ！里是北村第四景嘉洞31坡，屋聚集最多的地方。看到石外重重的屋瓦了如果看不太楚，着到石放的踏石上再看。不的瓦如波浪般形成柔美的曲覆盖整坡，是北村最具代表性的景点之一。步入上世30年代后，朝的官和族居住的大型屋然失去踪影，在土地上重新大模地建起了平民百姓居住的屋。在看到的屋密集的景象就是那始形成的。 在一座接一座的瓦中，是否有一座屋不同那就是“李俊九家宅”。座屋采用西式建筑格建造，屋是引人目的色。是一座典型的30年代朝上流人士的住宅，壁上使用的是取自朝南部城市城的花岩，用法瓦做以。按在的，就是用高口材料做了修。然在北村的好多大宅院已消失，但可以通李俊九家宅略一下豪世家的生活面貌。坡走下，前往北村第五景的路上，有一高大的槐，槐在方象征者和儒生。在朝代，槐尤其受到人喜，因槐繁茂的枝如者自由奔放地拓自己的域，所以人植槐祈今后家中出文官或者。分叉路走上去，就是北村第五景了。

오디오명: 北村最具代表性的屋聚集
대본내용: 我常在介北村的照片上看到的嘉洞31屋胡同是北村第五景、第六景和第七景的合点。在里可以比屋和北村屋的不同之。 在胡同下向上仰望，可看到北村第五景。里大部分使用的是大小均等的石，且有色花。里的屋不是用糊的，而是璃，而且有的上面又加了一扇。可是作上流人士居住的地方，些屋道不是太小了 是因北村的屋大部分是上世30年代建成的。朝王室的力逐衰退，族也失去官，走向落，所以以承大面房屋的，再加上工化的程，人口集中到首，就需要更多的房屋。于是，民建筑公司在原的地皮上重建了多座小面的屋，老百姓。因此，北村的屋模和外都大同小，且使用了璃和瓷等建筑材料，富有代格，但四面是房屋，中是院子一仍然保留下，可以保留了格，又融入了代要素，是一合代要求的新代屋。

如果沿着密集的屋走到坡的，不妨身看一眼。透的屋房看到立的高大厦。面是南山，山上的尖塔就是“N首塔”。N首塔的展望台可以一无余地看到首全景，因此每天都是人潮不、接踵而至。屋的隙中看到的代式高大厦，是不是有一番味道景就是北村第六景。 北村第七景是北村第六景右的胡同。因路只向右曲，的屋房如接在一起，石下有植着花草的花，十分可。 沿着北村第六景后方的胡同向上走可以到北村第八景。在去往北村第八景的路上有一得一去的地方，那就是“孟思古宅”址。在有有到“孟思古宅”石碑孟思是15世初朝的丞相，以廉、高的品德名于世，又是明文的世宗大王的老。 孟思朴素到每次出不坐子，而是牛，，他在音方面也有深的造，喜吹笛子。在，孟思古宅已成北村洋文化博物，列着和方各的珍文物。

스팟명: 北村第八景：三洞瞭望台
대본내용: 北村第八景是通往三洞的“石路”。石而下，到中止是代式台，但那后面就不是普通的台了。段矮矮的、看似平整的石，其是用一整岩石雕琢而成的。 窄的石，小胡同增添了一特的情致。 石端通往正的路上有“三洞瞭望台”，站在瞭望台上，可以望巨石踞的仁王山。仁王山原名“西山”，到世宗大王那一朝代改名“仁王山”。“仁王”在佛文中是“守神”的意思，含着守朝王朝的意。 仁王山下的景福是朝王朝的第一座王，由建立朝王朝的太祖李成桂指定了水地修建殿，在此定都。景福的北是府瓦台， 望着景福和瓦台，人不由想古都首600多年的悠久月。

오디오명: 幽的散步路着位王妃的故事
대본내용: 除了北村八景外，北村有多得一看的小巷，其中一就是适合散步的“感古堂路”。自安站到德成女子中止的路段有一道雅致的石，每到夏季，郁，爽宜人。石路的前往正止的路上坐落着家充活力的小店。然感古堂路不，但其史却不短。“感古堂”是朝的第19代王宗在1687年王妃仁王后的娘家建造的房屋，后由王后的父居住。仁王后在遭到宗的后禧陷害后，被黜平民逐出王，在此逗留了5年之久。 里被“感古堂”是久以后的事情了。宗和景宗之后登上王位的英祖，念仁王后，了。正如其名，“感古堂”意味着“感受古老往事的地方”。因仁王后有生育，曾英祖自己的生子百般照。孝的英祖是仁王后在此度月，生了大的怜。隔多年，感古堂又出了一位王妃，那就是朝第26代王高宗的王妃明成皇后。感古堂一直由仁王后的娘家氏家族居住，作氏家族后代的明成皇后在被封皇后之前，也在此生活了八年。今，感古堂已搬至明成皇后的故居所在的州，但其名仍然留在北村，述着感古堂同甘共苦的位王妃的人生。

오디오명: 市民的地，游客休息的地方
대본내용: 正成立于1977年，地北村路、三洞路和感古堂路的交，北村的中央。里除了的人，有多散步的人。里什如此受迎因每到春天，正就成一座花盛的花，而夏天可以在藤下欣泉。 在上世70年代，首市乎有，所以，在成立受到了烈的迎。了在，人凌晨始就排起的伍。那正是根于此的 据正的前身是京畿高中。步入上世70年代以后，江以南地起了潮，首的各大院校始搬到江南，京畿高中是第一搬象。但是校要搬的消息出后，却遭到在校生和生，乃至海外校友的强烈反。他心校原在北村享有高的，如果搬到江南，其悠久的史和有可能消失。后首市政府承，使校搬也不除校，而是其作使用，才得到大家同意。一，的、操和附施就全部原封不地保留下，成市民。 正旁的首育博物也的旅途增添一快。里列着公元4～7世到代的育文物，可以目睹育的史。按照代序，展出的科、校服和文具，原了校前的文具店。可以走放着和琴的室里面回想自己的生代。集中了首地各校校徽的展品也受迎。

北村除了屋以外，有多看点。其中之一就是充性的胡同。那，外人最喜的胡同是不，就是古老的桂洞路。 桂洞原名叫“生洞”，是朝代的救机生院的所在地。安站代大厦前就有“生院址”的石碑，而其旁是保健福利部的石碑，因保健福利部就位于代大厦里面。朝代的机址，在成百姓服的保健福利部，是史上妙的巧合。 桂洞路的景人想到上世7、80年代的首。小餐上的瓦屋，手的商店招牌已退色，全部都是古老的西。而吸引游客的正是道充愁的景。 桂洞路的地、北村第一家堂中央堂于1978年， 至今仍保留着的面貌。中央堂里的“金蛋餐”曾出在多篇里，名大震。桂洞路有迷般交叉的胡同，呈出在大路上看不到的首老百姓的日常生活景象。 桂洞路有多可以的屋和工房。如果想了解于屋寄宿的更多信息，可以到洞小前，法裁判所面的“屋生活服中心”取信息、文化。如果想更深入地了解北村，建在里住上一宿，。

三洞是首著名的旅游路之一，也是人常光的所。沿着景福石路散步，路的大小店，或去美食店，都是不的。三洞路然看上去和北村的其他地方有大的不同，但依是有古老史的北村的一部分。 “三洞”的名源于道的“三殿”。三殿是道供奉最高尊神三祖的殿堂。而里山、水秀、人品好，因此“三”是最合适不的名字。可是知道里曾被“三无村”，那是因里有便利店、店和霓虹，可是多的一地方。 在繁的市中心可能有些西首先要知道，位于景福、昌德和瓦台之的三洞出于文化保和警等，禁止。如今，在三洞周不警察的身影，是因附近有瓦台和理公。由于限制，三洞有高大厦，所存留的屋和小型建筑也都展出有的性。那，三洞何始成餐、店、廊和精品店聚集的文化一街了是1990年始解除限制起。江南的大型廊和已于和的仁寺洞的精品店一接一地搬到三洞，后光此地的客越越多，自然而然地形成了今天的三洞路。散步、和享受的好去三洞，定留下美好的回。
